title: Capacity-aware SLO relaxation with regional soft-eviction safeguard
preview: Context: We saw repeated cascade evictions during multi-region capacity spikes where strict SLO enforcement triggered aggressive circuit breakers, causing higher tail latency and customer throttles (SUP-9812). This PR introduces a capacity-aware, timebound SLO relaxation layer plus a regional soft-eviction safeguard that stages evictions to avoid synchronized mass-drains. What changed: 1) control/allocator: add 'capacity-aware slo_relaxation' policy that dynamically relaxes non-critical SLOs by a configurable percentage when sustained node-utilization exceeds a threshold for a hold window; 2) routing/eviction: introduce 'soft-eviction' state where instances are marked for probabilistic eviction over a decay window instead of immediate hard drain, enabling load to bleed off while respecting per-tenant priority buckets; 3) telemetry: new metrics (slo_relaxation.active, soft_eviction.pending, soft_eviction.success_rate) and traces for decision points; 4) ops: feature flag rts.slo_relaxation_v1 and runtime knobs for threshold/decay; 5) tests: end-to-end canary harness and unit coverage for decision logic. Motivation: allow graceful degradation that is both capacity- and cost-aware: we prefer slight SLO slack for low-priority requests over wholesale region failovers or bulk evictions which amplify disruption. Implementation notes: the relaxation algorithm is conservative by default (5% token latency budget increase, 2-minute hold window) and respects tenant-priority masks; the soft-eviction uses jittered timers and a token-bucket backoff to limit eviction rate per-region. Operation guidance: defaults are safe for most clusters; ops can tune threshold and relaxation_pct per-capacity class. Checklist: - [x] design doc linked (internal design doc: https://linear.app/ENG/1428/capacity-aware-slo-relaxation) - [x] unit tests - [x] integration canary in perf-lab - [x] metrics + dashboard patches - [x] feature-flag gating - [x] rollout playbook attached (see PR artifacts) Commits: 3 commits (initial implementation, metrics + tests, docs + rollback hooks). High-level changed files: control/allocator/policy_manager.go, control/allocator/slo_relaxation.go, routing/eviction/soft_eviction.go, routing/eviction/soft_eviction_test.go, telemetry/metrics_definitions.yaml, ops/feature_flags.yaml, docs/ops/slo-relaxation.md, examples/perf-canary/config.yml. Review conversation summary: Marcus Lin: "Can we make tenant-priority mask evaluation O(1) to avoid runtime cost?" — Author: "Updated mask cache to constant-time lookup and added microbenchmark (see commit 2)." Aisha Patel: "How do we avoid oscillation between relaxed and strict states under flapping load?" — Author: "Added hold window and exponential backoff on relaxation decay; documented rationale and added unit tests for flapping scenario." Ethan Zhao: "Suggest lowering default relaxation_pct to 3% for small regions." — Author: "Left default at 5% but exposed knob and wrote guidance; lowered default for regions with <8 GPUs in ops playbook." CI history: Initial run failed lint due to unused import in soft_eviction_test.go; fixed in commit 3. Integration perf canary showed reduced peak eviction rate in staging (40% -> 7%) with 1.8% median latency increase on low-priority traffic. Rollout plan: enable feature flag in staging for 24h, enable in one EU region with canary traffic split 5%, monitor slo_relaxation.active, soft_eviction.success_rate, region_eviction_rate and rollback in 30m if region_eviction_rate increases. Merge: squashed and merged after approvals from all reviewers and green CI. Post-merge actions: deploy to dedicated canary clusters, coordinate on-call rotation for first 48h, add alert thresholds to SLO dashboard. Notes: This is an operationally-sensitive change; although not a breaking API change, treat rollout as a minor incident risk and follow ops playbook.

title: Introduce transient request coalescer and SLO deceleration gate for burst resilience
preview:
Motivation: Short-lived client storms (retries/coordination spikes) can create sharp token-latency SLO violations because many identical or near-identical requests simultaneously hit the fastpath causing KV pressure and circuit flips. This PR introduces two cooperative mitigations: 1) a transient request coalescer at the front of the serving runtime that deduplicates identical requests arriving within a short configurable window (default 25ms) and coalesces them into a single upstream evaluation, and 2) an SLO deceleration gate which, instead of abruptly rejecting when SLO pressure is detected, reduces admission gradually via a short, measurable backoff window (configurable hysteresis) so clients see increased latency rather than immediate hard failures. Both features are behind a runtime feature flag and ship with tracing/metrics and a rollout plan.

What this PR contains:
- Transient coalescer: lightweight LRU of in-flight request signatures with a 25ms default coalescing window and max coalesced fan-in limit. Falls back to normal routing for non-coalescible requests (streaming / function-calls).
- SLO deceleration gate: admission controller that monitors the 95th/99th SLO buckets and applies a graded slowdown policy (token bucket-derived delay) per-tenant/route before hitting hard circuit thresholds. Includes per-tenant grace counters to avoid oscillation.
- Telemetry: new metrics (coalescer.coalesced_count, coalescer.dropped_fan_in, slo_gate.delay_ms, slo_gate.active_hysteresis, slo_gate.reason_tag) plus tracing annotations for coalesced requests and gate-induced delays.
- Config & flags: runtime config + console toggles + feature-flag gating for gradual rollout.
- Tests: unit tests for coalescer edge cases, property tests for gate hysteresis, and a small integration harness that simulates microbursts to validate reduced upstream load and smoother SLO curves.

Rollout plan:
1) Merge behind feature flag, default off.
2) Enable for <5% of routes in canary region with extended telemetry retention for a week.
3) Gradually increase to 50% while monitoring coalescer.fan_in and slo_gate.delay_ms and downstream error rates.

Related work: tackles the same symptom space as earlier load-shedding and circuit staging PRs but takes a softer approach (delay/coalesce vs immediate shed). Linked to ENG-1882 for SLO instrumentation scope and SRE-71 for runbook updates.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation + unit tests
- [x] Integration harness and load test results attached in comments
- [x] Feature flagging and console toggle added
- [x] Telemetry & tracing annotations added
- [x] Rollout instructions added to runbook

Notes: DoD requires verifying no increased memory growth under extreme coalescing (we cap entries and fan-in) and verifying multi-region semantics (coalescer is local to serving node; gate decisions are local but informed by global SLO metrics feed).

title: Add regression blame and diff-summary CI check for prompt-set evaluations
preview: Motivation: CI currently reports failing prompt-set metrics but gives limited context to quickly identify the source of a regression. This PR introduces a two-part CI hook: (1) regression-blame which correlates failing prompt cohorts to recent changes in prompt definitions, eval configs, and model pinning; (2) diff-summary which renders a compact human-readable summary of model-output changes for affected prompts. The goal is to reduce mean time-to-diagnose for eval regressions and to make gating decisions actionable. Implementation notes: regression-blame walks the prompt-set git history and the eval config commits in the last 30 days to produce candidate commit ranges; it then scores candidates by overlap with changed prompt IDs, changed tokenization metadata, and run-time seed/temperature differences. diff-summary runs a lightweight paired-output comparator that highlights token-level deltas, normalized scores, and a short semantic-change heuristic (BLEU/ROUGE delta + embedding cosine delta threshold). The CI hook plugs into existing eval GH actions and produces an artifact JSON and a Markdown summary comment. Checklist: [x] core algorithm and scoring, [x] GH action integration, [x] unit tests for scoring heuristics, [x] docs + runbook snippet, [x] small-scale perf bench on large prompt sets (< 10s per 100 prompts on CI runner). Related: Linear ENG-1882, ENG-2001. Jira: SUP-433 (customer report that prompted this work). Usage: when a gated eval fails, the CI will post a comment with a compact diff-summary and a ranked list of probable commits to inspect. The comment links to the full JSON artifact and a generated local html for quick triage. Notes for reviewers: the scoring weights are configurable via eval-config.yaml; defaults are conservative to avoid noisy blame; thresholds are documented. Rollout: implemented behind a feature flag eval_ci_blame enabled for 2 teams initially. This description contains implementation rationale, test plan, and rollout guidance in one continuous string.

title: Gateway: standardize request_id propagation across streaming + non-streaming errors
preview:
Context: SDK parity work (ENG-5120) and Support/Oncall feedback (INT-1182) highlighted that `request_id` is not reliably present across all failure modes. Specifically: (1) some gateway-generated errors returned `x-request-id` header but omitted `request_id` in JSON bodies, (2) upstream service errors sometimes used `requestId` or `trace_id` and the gateway passed them through inconsistently, and (3) streaming (SSE) error paths frequently dropped request_id entirely (disconnects, mid-stream 429/5xx, proxy-induced truncations), which made customer triage painful.

This PR standardizes request ID behavior end-to-end: the gateway guarantees a canonical request identifier exists for every request, propagates it to downstream services, and returns it consistently to the caller in both headers and error bodies/events.

What changed (high level):
- Canonicalization: adopt a single canonical field name `request_id` in all JSON error bodies and SSE error payloads.
- Header contract: gateway always sets `x-request-id` on responses (success + error). If a valid request id is already present in inbound `x-request-id`, we honor it; otherwise we generate one.
- Downstream propagation: gateway forwards `x-request-id` to downstream HTTP services and gRPC metadata so services can log it and (optionally) reflect it.
- Error normalization: map upstream variants (`requestId`, `trace_id`, `x-request-id`) into the canonical `request_id` field before returning to clients.
- Streaming: ensure the initial SSE metadata event includes `request_id`, and any SSE error event emitted by the gateway includes `request_id` even when the upstream stream fails mid-flight.

Why this is safe:
- No behavior change to success payload schemas.
- Adds fields/headers only; does not remove existing error fields (we keep legacy fields in internal logs only; client-facing remains canonicalized).
- Request IDs are non-secret; security review confirms they are safe to log and share with Support, and do not encode sensitive information.

Testing:
- Added unit tests for request-id selection/generation and upstream error normalization.
- Added integration tests for: JSON 429 with Retry-After, JSON 500, SSE mid-stream upstream 502, and gateway-generated 401/403 errors.

Follow-ups:
- SDKs will rely on this contract in their typed errors and conformance fixtures (ENG-5126 / ENG-5127).

title: silence-probe-reconnect-backfill-and-cancel-deadline-enforcement
preview: Motivation: Customers observed client disconnects that left server-side streaming state in a limbo where tool-calls were retried and KV-cache backfills were skipped, causing higher token costs and functional duplication. This PR introduces a 'silence probe' reconciliation path, a reconnect probe that attempts lightweight session health checks on client reconnects, an explicit cancel-deadline enforcement for in-flight tool/function calls, and safer backfill semantics when reconnect resumes. Summary of changes: 1) server: add silence-probe detection that marks streams as 'candidate-orphan' after configurable idle windows and enqueues a lightweight snapshot probe before evicting; 2) reconnect: on new client handshake include reconnect-probe phase which validates KV-cache continuity and replays missing prefix tokens if safe; 3) cancel-deadline: tool invocation subsystem now tracks cancel deadlines separately from client websocket state and enforces idempotent abort semantics with explicit response code for clients; 4) backfill behavior: when resuming, we prefer prefix-reconciliation over blind backfill and record a short reconciliation trace to observability; 5) OpenAI-compat: adapter layer now maps the reconnect-probe handshake into a 409/202-style flow when applicable and preserves function-call idempotency tokens. Implementation notes: code changes touch the session manager, rpc/stream controller, toolcall executor, and the OpenAI compatibility adapter. Default timeouts are conservative (idle->candidate-orphan = 45s, probe timeout = 5s, cancel-deadline grace = 2s) with knobs exposed in config. Tests: added unit tests for probe state transitions, e2e integration test that simulates abrupt client disconnect and reconnect with toolcalls, and property tests for idempotent abort on repeated cancel signals. Safety: probe performs non-destructive validation only and will not execute user-visible toolcalls during reconciliation. Rollout plan: feature gated via runtime flag 'streaming.silence_probe_v1' with spreadsheet of customers to flip; metrics added: stream.probe.started, stream.probe.success, stream.resume.reconciled, toolcall.abort.forced; alerting suggested on probe.failures > 1% over 10m. Backwards compatibility: existing client behavior unchanged unless server flag enabled. Related: follows ENG-4821 and references earlier streaming incident INT-998. Breaking change: none. See release_notes for end-user impact.

title: Wire telemetry events for Suggestions funnel + server-side API usage logging
preview: Context: For the Optimize Suggestions beta, we need a closed-loop funnel to understand which recommendations are useful and where users drop off (impression → expand → dismiss → apply-click). This PR wires client-side analytics events from the Console Suggestions list + details drawer, and adds server-side logging/metrics on the /optimize/suggestions API for usage + debugging (without collecting request/response bodies). Scope: (1) UI events: suggestions_impression, suggestion_expand, suggestion_dismiss, suggestion_apply_click; (2) API logs + counters: optimize_suggestions.list requests, result counts, latency buckets, authz denied; (3) shared schema helpers to keep event props consistent across UI and backend. Non-goals: no PII, no raw prompts/responses, no customer-level savings numbers in analytics events (those remain in product surfaces only). Event properties (UI): org_id (hashed), project_id (hashed), route_id (stable), model_id, suggestion_id (stable), suggestion_type (batching|caching), risk_level (low|medium|high), confidence_bucket (low|med|high), rank_in_list, list_sort, filter_state, region, ui_surface (suggestions_table|details_drawer). Event properties (API): org_id (hashed), project_id (hashed), endpoint (/optimize/suggestions), http_status_class, authz_result (allow|deny), page_size, returned_count, stale_as_of_present (true|false), sort_key. Privacy: org/project identifiers are hashed at the client boundary before emission; suggestion_id is a stable ID already surfaced in the UI and does not contain tenant-identifying info. Sampling: impressions sampled at 20% by default (configurable) to reduce volume; expand/dismiss/apply-click are unsampled. Rollout: behind feature flag optimize_suggestions_beta; events only emit when the flag is enabled and the Suggestions page is rendered. Testing: added unit tests for schema normalization, and Playwright coverage for emitting expand/dismiss/apply-click from the drawer. Related: aligns with dashboards work in observability-pack (PR #1843) and supports the beta funnel dashboard spec.

title: RBAC: add optimize.suggestions.read scope + enforcement for Suggestions API
preview: Context: the new Console Suggestions view calls the Optimize Suggestions API (list + details) and must respect org/project scoping and RBAC. This PR introduces a dedicated permission scope `optimize.suggestions.read` and enforces it server-side on `/v1/optimize/suggestions` and `/v1/optimize/suggestions/{id}`. Implementation details: (1) adds the scope to the authz policy bundle and wires it into the API gateway middleware, (2) ensures suggestions queries are always constrained to the caller’s org_id + optional project_id (no cross-tenant leakage), (3) returns a consistent 403 payload (`error_code=INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS`, `required_scope=optimize.suggestions.read`) that the Console can map to the RBAC denied empty state, and (4) emits audit log events for both allow/deny decisions with minimal fields (org_id, actor_id, resource_type=optimize_suggestions, action=read, decision, request_id) to support internal access investigations (see INT-11307). Tests: adds unit tests for policy evaluation, integration tests for the API handlers (403/200), and regression coverage for org boundary enforcement. Notes: this PR does not change feature-flag gating; it only ensures that once enabled, access is still permissioned. It also does not create a write/apply permission; that will be introduced separately when we add ‘apply suggestion’ actions.

Implementation details: (1) adds the scope to the authz policy bundle and wires it into the API gateway middleware, (2) ensures suggestions queries are always constrained to the caller’s org_id + optional project_id (no cross-tenant leakage), (3) returns a consistent 403 payload (`error_code=INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS`, `required_scope=optimize.suggestions.read`) that the Console can map to the RBAC denied empty state, and (4) emits audit log events for both allow/deny decisions with minimal fields (org_id, actor_id, resource_type=optimize_suggestions, action=read, decision, request_id) to support internal access investigations (see INT-11307). Tests: adds unit tests for policy evaluation, integration tests for the API handlers (403/200), and regression coverage for org boundary enforcement. Notes: this PR does not change feature-flag gating; it only ensures that once enabled, access is still permissioned. It also does not create a write/apply permission; that will be introduced separately when we add ‘apply suggestion’ actions.

title: tenant-prefill allocator + econ-sim CLI and quant-profile wizard
preview: Summary: Introduce a tenant-scoped PrefillAllocator that derives conservative prefill budgets, microbatch sizes, and transient burst lanes from tenant profile histograms; add econ-sim CLI that replays exported traces to estimate token-cost, median and tail latencies, and effective tokens-per-dollar for candidate defaults; include an interactive quantization profile wizard that generates three suggested qprofiles (safe, balanced, aggressive) with estimated quality delta and fallback rules. Motivation: Global defaults are brittle across tenant traffic shapes and lead to uneven unit economics. By simulating tenant-level outcomes and producing tenant-aware prefill and quant defaults, operators can reduce cost while containing regression risk. What changed: 1) runtime: pkg/prefill implements PrefillAllocator, tenant policy engine, and conservative safety multiplier; 2) cli: cmd/econ-sim provides replay tooling with S3/CSV inputs and outputs JSON/CSV summaries and suggested defaults; 3) tools: tools/quant-wizard generates qprofile artifacts and a small exportable manifest; 4) web: console preview page to review and apply tenant defaults behind feature flag; 5) telemetry: new sim events and metrics to track simulated cost and estimated quality delta; 6) db: migration adds tenant_profile defaults and a backfill job. Rollout: Feature-flagged under tenant_prefill_allocator_v1. Start internal alpha for 10 tenants, enable opt-in in Console, then staged rollout to opt-in customers. Safety: automatic fallback to global defaults if observed quality delta exceeds 3%; CLI and wizard default to conservative profile unless --allow-aggressive is specified. Tests: unit tests for allocator heuristics, integration tests for econ-sim replay, e2e smoke tests for console apply flow. Notes: Offline sim runs on representative traces showed balanced profiles reduce token-cost by ~12% with <1% simulated latency increase; aggressive profiles can approach ~20% saving but require manual review. CI: initial flaky integration test fixed (deterministic sort); final CI passed. Merge: squash-merged to main. Follow-ups: ENG-2499 to add Bayesian estimator for quality delta and additional telemetry dashboards.

title: gateway/errors: standardize error_code enum + docs for SDK retry classification
preview:
Context: As part of the cross-language SDK parity initiative (ENG-5126 / ENG-5130), SDKs need a stable way to classify errors as retryable vs terminal without relying on brittle string matching or status code heuristics alone. We currently have a mix of legacy `code` strings (inconsistent casing/names across services), missing codes in a few gateway paths, and doc drift. This PR introduces a single, documented `error_code` enum intended for SDK consumption and cleans up gateway mappings so the field is present consistently across JSON errors (including OpenAI-compat routes via the shared formatter).

What this PR does: (1) Defines a canonical `error_code` enum in the gateway error schema and updates the error formatter to always emit `error_code`, `message`, `type`, `request_id`, `status_code`, and `retryable`. (2) Adds mapping logic from downstream service error payloads and legacy fields into the canonical enum (with conservative fallback to `UNKNOWN`). (3) Aligns 429/503/504 behaviors with the retry standard: 429 includes `Retry-After` (handled separately in PR-28411) and now uses `RATE_LIMITED`; 503/504 map to `SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE`/`GATEWAY_TIMEOUT` with `retryable=true`. (4) Documents the enum and retry semantics in the API docs (internal spec + developer-facing references used by SDK teams).

Non-goals: This does not change rate limiting policy, billing behavior, model semantics, or introduce new auth mechanisms. We also intentionally do not promise a 1:1 mapping between HTTP status and retryability; SDKs should prefer the `retryable` boolean + `error_code` for user messaging and analytics.

Error fields (shape): `{"error": {"type": "api_error", "error_code": "RATE_LIMITED", "message": "...", "status_code": 429, "request_id": "req_...", "retryable": true, "details": {...}}}`. Notes: `details` remains service-specific and is not part of the stability contract.

Enum values added/standardized (initial set): RATE_LIMITED, SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE, GATEWAY_TIMEOUT, REQUEST_TIMEOUT, INTERNAL, BAD_REQUEST, UNAUTHORIZED, FORBIDDEN, NOT_FOUND, CONFLICT, PAYLOAD_TOO_LARGE, UNSUPPORTED_MEDIA_TYPE, UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY, CONTEXT_LENGTH_EXCEEDED, MODEL_NOT_FOUND, MODEL_UNAVAILABLE, QUOTA_EXCEEDED, INVALID_API_KEY, INVALID_BEARER_TOKEN, POLICY_VIOLATION, STREAMING_DISCONNECTED, UNKNOWN.

Retry semantics: (a) `retryable=true` for RATE_LIMITED, SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE, GATEWAY_TIMEOUT, REQUEST_TIMEOUT, INTERNAL (only when originating from gateway/infra), and STREAMING_DISCONNECTED when the gateway detects an upstream disconnect pre-first-token; (b) `retryable=false` for auth errors, validation errors, policy violations, and quota exceeded. (c) SDKs should still implement idempotency-safe retry guidance for streaming; the error code exists primarily for classification + user messaging.

Docs: Added/updated an "Error codes" section with a compatibility note: new field `error_code` is the stable contract; legacy `code` remains for now but is deprecated in docs. Included examples for JSON errors and OpenAI-compat error format.

Testing: Unit tests cover mapping table + formatter output; integration-style tests cover (1) 429 with Retry-After header still present, (2) downstream error passthrough/mapping, and (3) OpenAI-compat route formatting. Also validated that `request_id` is populated (ties into PR-28426 for broader request_id propagation).

Rollout: Safe to ship behind existing gateway deployment. No client break expected; SDKs can begin consuming `error_code` immediately, and we can later deprecate legacy `code` when all SDKs are updated.

Related: PR-28411 (Retry-After on 429), PR-28426 (request_id propagation), redwood-openai-compat PR-433 (align compat layer error shapes).

Checklist: [x] Updated docs/spec references used by SDK teams [x] Added tests for mapping + formatting [x] Security reviewed for no sensitive data added to error payloads [x] Coordinated with SDK parity owners (Ibrahim) for enum names/fields.

title: Add custodian provenance index and region-pin assertor for KMS-backed RBAC v2
preview: Context: For customer audits and regulatory evidence we need deterministic provenance for RBAC v2 decisions and stronger enforcement that prevents CMKs from being used outside their declared geozone. This PR adds two pieces: (1) a lightweight Policy Provenance Index (PPI) that records the canonical fingerprint of a policy decision, inputs (claims, operator), and the KMS key identifier used to encrypt the envelope; and (2) a Region-Pin Assertor that enforces a mapping between tenant/residency claims and KMS key zone attributes. Motivation: current audit logs are verbose but lack reliable deduplication and a canonical decision identifier; KMS consumption has occasional misrouted keys for tenants with strict data residency requirements. Solution summary: - Introduce a PPI service that runs as an in-process index (persisted to consistency-store) and exposes a hashed decision id (ppi_id) that is emitted in audit envelopes and API responses. - Wire ppi_id generation into the authorization flow so every RBAC v2 decision includes the provenance id. - Add a RegionPinAssertor middleware in the kms allocator that validates key.zone against tenant.residency_claim and rejects or routes to fallback KMSSafe pools. - Add a feature flag (feature.provenance_index.enabled) and staged canary config (10% of traffic) for rollout. - Add tooling to backfill provenance ids for recent audit windows and a reconciliation job to reconcile index vs audit ledger. Checklist: - [x] Design doc: Linear ENG-8421, link: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-8421/policy-provenance-index - [x] Unit tests for ppi hashing and deterministic serialization - [x] Integration test covering RBAC decision -> audit envelope -> kpi emission - [x] E2E gating with region-pin enabled in staging - [x] Feature-flag guarded rollout + migration tool - [x] Add console metrics: ppi_emit_rate, region_pin_rejections Open items: - Reconciliation job tuning parameters need ops sign-off (added TODO in ops playbook). - UX for showing ppi_id in Console is an upcoming follow-up (PM-163). Notable implementation details: - Provenance id is SHA256 over canonicalized tuple: (policy_fingerprint, sorted_claims, operator_id, timestamp_truncated_to_minute). This balances uniqueness vs noise and avoids per-request non-determinism. - Persisted index uses existing tenancy-sharded store (no new infra). We added TTL 90d with manual retention policy controlled via config. - Region pin uses service-level mapping config with static geozone -> allowed kms pools, and a soft-fail mode that logs and emits an audit alert instead of rejecting. Usage example snippet (emitted audit envelope): {"timestamp":"2026-02-18T15:12:34Z","actor":"operator:svc-claims-338","ppi_id":"ppi:3f2a9b...","decision":"ALLOW","kms_key":"cmk:aws:us-west-2:...","tenant_residency":"EU"} Migration notes: - Backfill tool scans last 7d of audit envelopes and creates PPI entries when missing (idempotent). - A reconciliation report will surface mismatches to SRE for manual review. Rollout plan: - Canary 10% for 48h, monitor ppi_emit_rate and region_pin_rejections - Increase to 50% for 24h, then 100% if metrics stable - Post-rollout, run reconciliation and enable retention policy Default behavior is opt-in; customers with strict residency will have region-pin forced via tenant flag. See docs in docs/security/provenance.md for operator instructions.

title: Add CI lint for audit event required fields + event_type registry enforcement
preview:
Context
We’re standardizing audit logging across Hosted/Dedicated/Private and aiming for 100% coverage for sensitive actions. We’ve now got the schema library + validator and a centralized event_type registry, but we don’t have enforcement at the boundary where producers get added/changed. This PR introduces a CI gate that prevents:
- emitting an unregistered event_type
- emitting an event missing required fields (per schema v1 + sensitive-action taxonomy requirements)
- re-introducing previously fixed omissions during refactors

What’s in this PR
- Adds a new repo tool: tools/auditlint
  - Parses audit event emission callsites (current patterns: audit.Emit(...) and audit.NewEvent(...).Emit(...))
  - Validates event_type against the centralized registry introduced in pr-21991
  - Validates required fields for the event_type against the v1 field rules (schema + taxonomy requirements)
  - Enforces a small set of cardinality safety rules for a few known footguns (e.g., disallow raw request/response bodies in extensions)
- Adds CI integration
  - New Buildkite step: "auditlint" running on PRs that touch server/*, services/*, console-backend/*, private/*, or tooling that emits audit events
  - Outputs GitHub PR annotations for missing fields + unknown event_type
  - Provides an escape hatch only via a checked-in allowlist file with an expiry date + a link to an approved exception (see below)
- Adds developer guidance
  - docs/standards/audit-logging.md: how to add a new event_type, how to run the linter locally, common failures, and examples
  - docs/standards/audit-logging.md also clarifies correlation fields expectations (request_id/trace_id/admin_action_id) but does not introduce new requirements beyond schema v1

Rules enforced (initial set)
- Unknown event_type is a hard error
- Required common fields are a hard error:
  - schema_version
  - event_id
  - occurred_at
  - tenant_id
  - actor.type + actor.id (or actor.type=system with actor.id)
  - source.ip (when present in request context; linter only asserts presence for HTTP handlers, not for async jobs)
- Per-event required fields are enforced for the sensitive-action set (ENG-4822 taxonomy), e.g.:
  - rbac.role.updated requires target.type=role, target.id, and change_summary
  - api_key.created requires target.type=api_key, target.id, and redaction marker (no key material)

Exception mechanism
- If a callsite cannot be linted (generated code, or transitional legacy producer), add an entry to:
  - tools/auditlint/allowlist.txt
  - Each line must include an expiry date and a tracking issue (Linear/Jira)
- CI fails if an allowlist entry is expired

Why a callsite-based lint (vs runtime-only)
Runtime-only validation catches malformed payloads but does not guarantee we never ship a codepath that omits required context for a sensitive action. The callsite lint is meant to be a cheap, early warning signal. It’s intentionally conservative in v1 and scoped to known emission patterns.

Rollout plan
- This PR lands with the lint enabled in "warn" mode for main branch builds only (nightly) for 48h, then flips to required on PRs.
- We’ve already fixed the known existing failures in a follow-up diff on this branch (mostly missing source.ip in a couple admin routes + a legacy event_type typo).

Testing
- Unit tests for auditlint parsing + rule evaluation
- Golden tests for annotation output
- Verified on a sample change set touching RBAC + API key endpoints

Notes
- This linter does not attempt to validate runtime values (e.g., that actor.id is a valid UUID), only presence/shape.
- Structured payload redaction is still handled by the event library; this lint only blocks obvious secret/PII logging patterns in extensions.

Related
- ENG-4822 / pr-21991: event_type registry
- pr-21984: audit event schema library + validator
- ENG-4830 / pr-22047: correlation IDs standardization

title: api-gateway: consistently emit Retry-After on 429 responses + align SDK error fields
preview: Context: As part of the SDK parity initiative (Python/TS/Go), we need the API gateway to reliably communicate backpressure so clients can respect server guidance and avoid retry storms. Today, 429 responses are inconsistent: some routes include Retry-After, others do not; some error bodies omit fields that SDKs use for typed error classification (code/request_id). This PR makes Retry-After emission deterministic for all 429s generated by the gateway rate limiter and harmonizes the JSON error shape consumed by the SDKs. Changes: (1) Always attach a Retry-After header on gateway-generated 429 responses. The value is computed from the limiter reset/next-allowed time and is emitted as delta-seconds (integer, min 1s). (2) Ensure error responses include consistent top-level and nested fields: status, error.code, error.message, error.type, request_id. (3) Add tests covering Retry-After presence/format across REST + streaming handshake paths (i.e., headers returned before body streaming begins) and ensure request_id is propagated. (4) Add metrics + structured logs to support rollout monitoring: gateway_429_total, gateway_429_retry_after_seconds histogram, and logs including request_id, route, customer_id (hashed), and computed retry_after_seconds. Notes/decisions: We intentionally emit delta-seconds (not HTTP-date) to keep SDK parsing uniform; SDKs already support both formats, but we want a single standard server-side. We only set Retry-After for 429 at this time; 503s due to overload remain unchanged (separate follow-up tracked). For pass-through 429s from downstream services, we preserve an existing Retry-After if present, but do not synthesize one (to avoid conflicting guidance). Security: no sensitive identifiers added to logs; customer_id is hashed and request headers are not logged. Rollout: safe to deploy behind the gateway canary first; SRE to monitor 429 rate + retry behavior dashboards during SDK beta. Testing: unit tests for limiter computation + integration tests validating headers and JSON shape. Related: redwood-sdk-* PRs use Retry-After if present and will behave more predictably after this lands.

(4) Add metrics + structured logs to support rollout monitoring: gateway_429_total, gateway_429_retry_after_seconds histogram, and logs including request_id, route, customer_id (hashed), and computed retry_after_seconds. Notes/decisions: We intentionally emit delta-seconds (not HTTP-date) to keep SDK parsing uniform; SDKs already support both formats, but we want a single standard server-side. We only set Retry-After for 429 at this time; 503s due to overload remain unchanged (separate follow-up tracked). For pass-through 429s from downstream services, we preserve an existing Retry-After if present, but do not synthesize one (to avoid conflicting guidance). Security: no sensitive identifiers added to logs; customer_id is hashed and request headers are not logged. Rollout: safe to deploy behind the gateway canary first; SRE to monitor 429 rate + retry behavior dashboards during SDK beta. Testing: unit tests for limiter computation + integration tests validating headers and JSON shape. Related: redwood-sdk-* PRs use Retry-After if present and will behave more predictably after this lands.

title: Audit events for retention + data residency configuration changes (before/after summaries)
preview:
## Summary
This PR instruments the control plane settings surfaces for (a) retention policy changes and (b) data residency / region restrictions changes to emit canonical audit events (schema v1).

The main goal is to close the gap for “sensitive actions” around data handling controls so that Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments produce equivalent audit signals and customers can prove who changed retention/residency settings and what changed.

## Context
- Linear: ENG-4839 (data residency + retention audit events)
- Related: schema v1 + required fields (ENG-4821), redaction rules (ENG-4832), correlation IDs (ENG-4830)
- Customer pressure: SUP-18542 (security review questions on retention/residency auditability)

## What changed
### New/updated audit event types (v1)
Adds audit emission for the following configuration mutations:
- `data.retention.updated`
- `data.residency.updated`

Both event types are registered in the centralized audit registry and validate against the v1 schema.

### Actor/target semantics
- `actor.type`: `user` (Console) or `service` (automation / internal jobs)
- `actor.id`: internal principal id
- `actor.display_name`: only when available from auth context (kept bounded)
- `actor.impersonated_by`: populated when a support/admin session is used (if present in request context)
- `source.ip`: set from gateway-forwarded headers (best effort)

Targets:
- `target.type`: `tenant` or `project` depending on scope
- `target.id`: stable UUID
- `resource.type`: `retention_policy` or `residency_policy`
- `resource.id`: stable config object id (not human names)

### Before/after summaries (cardinality-safe)
We include a bounded, normalized summary of changes in `change_summary` rather than dumping raw configs:
- `change_summary.fields_changed`: comma-separated allow-listed field names
- `change_summary.before`: normalized key/value string (bounded; values truncated)
- `change_summary.after`: normalized key/value string (bounded; values truncated)
- `change_summary.diff`: compact diff string with capped length

Notes:
- We do *not* emit arbitrary JSON blobs from request bodies.
- We do *not* include customer-provided free-form strings (e.g., project display names).

### Redaction & data classification
- Explicitly excludes secrets (none expected in these endpoints, but we still guard against accidental logging of headers/body).
- Retention duration values are logged as integer days (or `disabled`).
- Residency settings logged only as enumerated regions / policy mode; no IP ranges, no customer endpoints.

### Correlation IDs
- Plumbs `request_id` and `trace_id` from request context into emitted events.
- Adds `admin_action_id` for Console-initiated mutations (generated once per request in Console backend and passed through).

## Implementation notes
- Audit emission is performed after config validation and persistence, but before response is returned.
- If audit sink is unavailable, we follow current “best effort with buffered retry” semantics (no user-facing failure) and emit a metric + structured log for SRE triage.

## Test plan
- Unit tests: change-summary normalization, allow-list enforcement, truncation behavior.
- Integration tests: PATCH retention + residency endpoints produce an audit event that validates against v1 schema.
- Manual: verified in staging by changing retention from 30d -> 90d and residency from `US_ONLY` -> `US_EU` and confirming the exporter payload includes `schema_version=v1`.

## Example events (sanitized)
Event: `data.retention.updated`
- `change_summary.fields_changed=retention_days,retention_mode`
- `change_summary.before=retention_mode=enabled;retention_days=30`
- `change_summary.after=retention_mode=enabled;retention_days=90`

Event: `data.residency.updated`
- `change_summary.fields_changed=residency_mode,allowed_regions`
- `change_summary.before=residency_mode=restricted;allowed_regions=us-east-1`
- `change_summary.after=residency_mode=restricted;allowed_regions=us-east-1,eu-west-1`

## Rollout
Safe to ship behind existing audit-event emission flags. No schema changes; uses v1 fields only. Exporter normalization not required for these new events.

## Follow-ups
- Add a coverage check in ENG-4828 dashboard to ensure these events are observed for all control plane services that expose retention/residency surfaces.
- Document customer-facing semantics in the Security FAQ update (PM-939).

title: Add Security FAQ / RFP snippets to Internal Admin (approved-language library + API)
preview: Summary: This PR adds first-class “Security Snippets” support to Internal Admin so we can centrally manage approved FAQ/RFP text blocks (and their versions) that Sales/SE tooling consumes. This is part of the Enterprise Security Enablement Kit refresh: we’ve had repeated drift between Drive/Confluence snippets, HubSpot templates, and ad-hoc copy/paste. The goal is a single source of truth with clear ownership, review metadata, and safe distribution semantics (no restricted evidence in snippets). Motivation / context: Enterprise security reviews are gating Dedicated/Private deals; field teams need fast, consistent answers without over-claiming. Today, approved language lives across Confluence + Drive and gets copied into various tools. This introduces: (1) a canonical snippet store; (2) an API for downstream consumption; (3) RBAC + audit log events for changes; (4) explicit versioning + “last reviewed” metadata to align with enablement kit governance. Scope (what’s included): - New snippet domain model + persistence: `security_snippets` table (Postgres) with: `key` (stable identifier), `title`, `category`, `deployment_modes` (Hosted/Dedicated/Private flags stored as text), `content_markdown`, `status` (draft|in_review|approved|deprecated), `version`, `last_reviewed_at`, `expires_at` (optional), `owner` (team/alias), `approver` (name), and `notes_internal` (e.g., references to evidence request playbook). - Versioning semantics: update creates a new version row (immutable content by version). “Approved” is per-version; deprecating a version does not delete it. - Internal Admin UI: new “Security & Compliance → Snippets” page with: list + filters (category, status, deployment mode), diff viewer between versions, and an “export” view (raw markdown or plaintext) for downstream tools. - Public/consumption API (internal): `GET /internal/snippets/security?status=approved` and `GET /internal/snippets/security/{key}`. The API returns only non-restricted fields; explicitly excludes `notes_internal` and any future evidence references. - Permissions: gated behind Internal Admin RBAC permission `admin.security_snippets.write` (write) and `admin.security_snippets.read` (read). Default: read for Sales Ops + Solutions Eng; write limited to Security/GRC + Product Security PM. (See reviewers’ notes below re: default grants.) - Audit logging: emits `security.snippet.created`, `security.snippet.updated`, `security.snippet.status_changed` with actor, snippet key, version, and timestamp. These show up in existing audit log pipelines for internal systems. - Seeded initial snippet keys: a small curated set to validate workflow (SSO/SAML, SCIM, RBAC granularity, audit log retention/export, data use/no-training language, encryption in transit/at rest, SOC 2 report request steps). The exact text was copied from the working GTM draft library and then reviewed in this PR; it is not intended to be a complete library in this change. Out of scope / explicit non-goals: - No new product features or promises; no roadmap language. - No restricted compliance evidence (SOC 2 report, pen test reports, vuln scan artifacts) is stored here. Snippets may include the approved process for requesting evidence under NDA, but not the evidence itself. - No direct HubSpot/CRM integration in this PR; the first consumer is the generic response tool (tracked separately) and manual use via export. Implementation details: - Backend: `internal-admin-api` adds `SecuritySnippetService` + repository; migrations in `migrations/2026_02_07_security_snippets.sql`. - Frontend: `internal-admin-ui` adds `SecuritySnippetsPage`, `SnippetEditor` (markdown), and `VersionDiffModal`. - API response includes `content_markdown` and `content_text` (server-side stripped markdown) to reduce divergence between tools that support rich text vs plain text. - All endpoints require admin auth; responses include `etag` and support `If-None-Match` to reduce polling load for downstream tooling. Changed areas (high level): - `services/internal-admin-api/src/security_snippets/*` - `services/internal-admin-api/src/routes/internal/snippets_security.ts` - `services/internal-admin-ui/src/pages/security/SecuritySnippetsPage.tsx` - `packages/rbac/policies/internal-admin.yaml` - `packages/audit-events/src/events/security_snippets.ts` - DB migration and seeds under `services/internal-admin-api/db/*` Example snippet (illustrative, shortened): Key: `sso_saml_scim_overview` Title: “SSO (SAML) + SCIM support” Category: “Access Controls” Content (excerpt): “Redwood supports SAML 2.0 SSO for the Redwood Console and supports SCIM 2.0 for user provisioning with supported IdPs. Role-based access control (RBAC) is enforced for console actions and API keys. For details on supported IdPs and RBAC roles, see the internal capability reference. If a requirement is not currently supported, follow the Security Questionnaire Exceptions Policy.” Test plan: - Unit tests: service + repository (create, new version on update, status transitions) - API tests: auth required; status filter; ETag behavior - UI smoke test: create draft (GRC/Sec role), request review, approve, view diff - Verified audit events appear in internal audit log stream for a status change - Manual: export approved snippets as plaintext and paste into a sample RFP questionnaire section (formatting preserved reasonably) Security / compliance considerations: - RBAC gating prevents broad write access; all mutations are audited. - “No evidence stored” is enforced by process + UI copy: editor includes a warning banner and blocks uploads/attachments. - PII: none expected; we added a lint check in editor that warns (non-blocking) if content appears to include email addresses or phone numbers. Review conversation (highlights): - Marcus Lin (Security Eng): requested audit event fields include `snippet_key` + `version` and that the update path is versioned (not in-place). Addressed in commit `a8b3f2c` (adds `version` and immutability). Approved after changes. - Olga Petrov (GRC): requested stronger do-not-say guardrails and explicit “evidence request under NDA” language in SOC 2 snippet; also requested that ISO language avoids implying certification. Updated seeded snippets and added an `approved_claims_tags` field (string list stored as comma-separated text) to support future reporting; tags are not yet exposed to consumers. Approved. - Naomi Feldman (Product Security PM): requested that deployment mode filters be explicit (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) and that any roadmap mention be removed from seeded audit logging snippet. Updated copy and added deployment mode UI chips. Approved. - Camila Reyes (Product Marketing): requested consistency with security messaging pillars (terminology: “Hosted API / Dedicated / Private”) and removal of ambiguous “industry standard” claims in the generic encryption snippet. Updated snippet text accordingly. Approved. - Markus Klein (Solutions Eng): requested plaintext export option and that snippets be searchable by common questionnaire phrasing (“Do you train on customer data?”, “SIEM export”, “SCIM”). Added full-text search across title/content and `keywords` field (stored as text). Approved. CI status: - Build: pass - Unit tests: pass - Lint: pass - Migration check: pass - UI build: pass Rollout / ops notes: - Feature is behind existing Internal Admin access; new page visible only with `admin.security_snippets.read`. - Seed data runs in staging only; production will start empty and be populated by Security/GRC after the enablement kit review cycle. - Follow-up: wire the generic response tool to this API and enforce “approved only” in consumers (tracked in INT-1234 + PM-4829 follow-up tasks).

title: Add integration tests: sensitive actions emit v1 audit events
preview:
Context: As part of the audit logging standardization work, we need automated verification that every “sensitive action” emits a canonical v1 audit event (and that the payload validates) across Hosted and Private. This PR adds end-to-end integration tests that execute representative sensitive actions and assert that: (1) an audit event is produced, (2) event_type matches the taxonomy registry, (3) required fields are present and schema_version is set, and (4) correlation fields (request_id/trace_id/admin_action_id) are populated and consistent. Motivation: We have active customer escalations where coverage is hard to prove quickly (SUP-18421, SUP-18477). These tests are intended to become part of our “100% coverage” gate and to provide fast signal on regressions when new admin flows are added.

What’s included: Test harness: A new audit-event test helper that polls the audit ingest test sink with bounded retry and supports filtering by (tenant_id, actor.user_id, admin_action_id) to avoid flakiness and high-cardinality queries. Hosted fixture: Runs against the standard integration environment (api-gateway + control-plane + console-backend) using a service account to perform admin operations. Private fixture: Runs against the “private-mode” docker-compose test stack (single-node control plane + local audit sink) to ensure parity on event emission and schema tagging. Assertions: JSON Schema validation for v1 (via the shared validator library introduced earlier) plus targeted assertions for actor/target semantics (actor_type, actor_id, actor_session_id, source_ip where available). Correlation: Ensures request_id and trace_id are present; when an action spans multiple internal calls, the tests assert all emitted events share the same admin_action_id.

Sensitive actions covered (initial set): RBAC: Create role, update role permissions, assign role to user, remove role assignment. API Keys: Create key, rotate key, revoke key (asserts key material is never present; only key_id + truncated display_name). Policy/Routing: Update routing policy (fallback config) and verify event includes a stable policy_id and a bounded diff_summary. Export Config: Update audit log export destination (tests only the control-plane config write path; UI validation is covered elsewhere).

Not included / follow-ups: We intentionally did not add “data residency/retention change” tests in this PR to keep runtime manageable; those will land with the residency event producers (ENG-4839) and will reuse this harness. We also do not enforce “exactly one event per action” because some actions legitimately emit multiple events (e.g., policy change + rollout application); instead we assert at least one matching primary event_type and that any additional events validate.

Implementation details: The tests use a new TestAuditSinkClient with exponential backoff (max 30s) and a deterministic query key built from admin_action_id. We tag each test action with an x-admin-action-id header generated once per test and propagated through the request context (requires prior propagation work from ENG-4830; this PR includes small glue in test clients only). Where source_ip is not reliably present in local/private runs, the test asserts that source_ip is either a valid IP string OR omitted, but it must never be a free-form value. The schema validator runs in “strict required fields + allow namespaced extensions” mode so teams can add x_redwood_* extensions without breaking tests.

Changed areas (high level): packages/audit/audit-schema-validator: add a test-friendly entrypoint for validating a decoded payload and returning a concise error list (no stack traces) packages/audit/audit-testkit: new helper for polling the sink and matching events by predicate services/control-plane/tests/audit_sensitive_actions_test.go: RBAC + API key lifecycle scenarios services/policy-engine/tests/audit_policy_change_test.go: routing policy update scenario services/audit-ingest/test-sink: adds support for querying by admin_action_id with TTL-limited in-memory index (test-only build tag) ci/integration: wires new suite into nightly + gated “security” job (gated on files under services/control-plane, services/policy-engine, packages/audit)

Sample assertion (illustrative): Expect event_type == "rbac.role.updated"; schema_version == "1.0"; event_id is non-empty; occurred_at is RFC3339; actor_type == "user" (or "service_account" in fixtures); actor_id matches requester; tenant_id matches; request_id/trace_id present; admin_action_id equals injected header; target_type == "rbac_role"; target_id equals created role id; diff_summary length < 1024 and contains only allowlisted keys; no secret-like fields appear (regex guard against token/key/pem/private_key).

CI: - unit: pass - lint: pass - integration-hosted (new suite): pass - integration-private (new suite): pass Note: integration-private initially failed due to the test sink index not being enabled under the correct build tag; fixed in commit 6/7.

Risk/rollback: Low risk. Changes are test-only except for the test-sink query index behind a test build tag. If we see flakes, we can temporarily move these tests to nightly-only by toggling the CI job condition, but the intent is to keep them gated for sensitive areas.

Docs/ops: Adds a short README to packages/audit/audit-testkit describing how to run locally and how to add new sensitive action cases. Follow-up: Logan is adding a coverage dashboard view that correlates expected event_types to observed events; these tests will be one of the inputs.

Checklist: [x] Tests cover both Hosted and Private fixtures [x] Validates payload against schema v1 [x] Asserts taxonomy-compliant event_type [x] Guards against secrets/PII leakage in payload [x] CI wired with stable runtime (< 9 minutes for new suite)

Refs: Linear ENG-4834. Related: PR-21984 (schema validator), PR-21991 (event registry), PR-22047 (correlation). Customer context: SUP-18421, SUP-18477.

Motivation: We have active customer escalations where coverage is hard to prove quickly (SUP-18421, SUP-18477). These tests are intended to become part of our “100% coverage” gate and to provide fast signal on regressions when new admin flows are added.

What’s included: Test harness: A new audit-event test helper that polls the audit ingest test sink with bounded retry and supports filtering by (tenant_id, actor.user_id, admin_action_id) to avoid flakiness and high-cardinality queries. Hosted fixture: Runs against the standard integration environment (api-gateway + control-plane + console-backend) using a service account to perform admin operations. Private fixture: Runs against the “private-mode” docker-compose test stack (single-node control plane + local audit sink) to ensure parity on event emission and schema tagging. Assertions: JSON Schema validation for v1 (via the shared validator library introduced earlier) plus targeted assertions for actor/target semantics (actor_type, actor_id, actor_session_id, source_ip where available). Correlation: Ensures request_id and trace_id are present; when an action spans multiple internal calls, the tests assert all emitted events share the same admin_action_id.

Sensitive actions covered (initial set): RBAC: Create role, update role permissions, assign role to user, remove role assignment. API Keys: Create key, rotate key, revoke key (asserts key material is never present; only key_id + truncated display_name). Policy/Routing: Update routing policy (fallback config) and verify event includes a stable policy_id and a bounded diff_summary. Export Config: Update audit log export destination (tests only the control-plane config write path; UI validation is covered elsewhere).

Not included / follow-ups: We intentionally did not add “data residency/retention change” tests in this PR to keep runtime manageable; those will land with the residency event producers (ENG-4839) and will reuse this harness. We also do not enforce “exactly one event per action” because some actions legitimately emit multiple events (e.g., policy change + rollout application); instead we assert at least one matching primary event_type and that any additional events validate.

Implementation details: The tests use a new TestAuditSinkClient with exponential backoff (max 30s) and a deterministic query key built from admin_action_id. We tag each test action with an x-admin-action-id header generated once per test and propagated through the request context (requires prior propagation work from ENG-4830; this PR includes small glue in test clients only). Where source_ip is not reliably present in local/private runs, the test asserts that source_ip is either a valid IP string OR omitted, but it must never be a free-form value. The schema validator runs in “strict required fields + allow namespaced extensions” mode so teams can add x_redwood_* extensions without breaking tests.

Changed areas (high level): packages/audit/audit-schema-validator: add a test-friendly entrypoint for validating a decoded payload and returning a concise error list (no stack traces) packages/audit/audit-testkit: new helper for polling the sink and matching events by predicate services/control-plane/tests/audit_sensitive_actions_test.go: RBAC + API key lifecycle scenarios services/policy-engine/tests/audit_policy_change_test.go: routing policy update scenario services/audit-ingest/test-sink: adds support for querying by admin_action_id with TTL-limited in-memory index (test-only build tag) ci/integration: wires new suite into nightly + gated “security” job (gated on files under services/control-plane, services/policy-engine, packages/audit)

Sample assertion (illustrative): Expect event_type == "rbac.role.updated"; schema_version == "1.0"; event_id is non-empty; occurred_at is RFC3339; actor_type == "user" (or "service_account" in fixtures); actor_id matches requester; tenant_id matches; request_id/trace_id present; admin_action_id equals injected header; target_type == "rbac_role"; target_id equals created role id; diff_summary length < 1024 and contains only allowlisted keys; no secret-like fields appear (regex guard against token/key/pem/private_key).

CI: - unit: pass - lint: pass - integration-hosted (new suite): pass - integration-private (new suite): pass Note: integration-private initially failed due to the test sink index not being enabled under the correct build tag; fixed in commit 6/7.

Risk/rollback: Low risk. Changes are test-only except for the test-sink query index behind a test build tag. If we see flakes, we can temporarily move these tests to nightly-only by toggling the CI job condition, but the intent is to keep them gated for sensitive areas.

Docs/ops: Adds a short README to packages/audit/audit-testkit describing how to run locally and how to add new sensitive action cases. Follow-up: Logan is adding a coverage dashboard view that correlates expected event_types to observed events; these tests will be one of the inputs.

Checklist: [x] Tests cover both Hosted and Private fixtures [x] Validates payload against schema v1 [x] Asserts taxonomy-compliant event_type [x] Guards against secrets/PII leakage in payload [x] CI wired with stable runtime (< 9 minutes for new suite)

Refs: Linear ENG-4834. Related: PR-21984 (schema validator), PR-21991 (event registry), PR-22047 (correlation). Customer context: SUP-18421, SUP-18477.

title: Usage insights: async CSV export endpoint + signed downloads + audit events
preview:
Summary: Adds server-side CSV export for in-console usage insights (route/model breakdown) as an async job with signed downloads and audit logging. This is intended for the Usage/Cost Insights beta flow: user triggers export from the console, export is generated in the background, and the UI polls for completion before presenting a signed download link.

Motivation: Customers and internal finance workflows need shareable/exportable reports that match billing totals. Doing this synchronously from the query endpoints was timing out for 7d/30d ranges and made it hard to enforce consistent limits and auditability.

What’s included: (1) New Export API (v1): POST /v1/usage_insights/exports creates an export job; GET /v1/usage_insights/exports/{export_id} returns job status + metadata; POST /v1/usage_insights/exports/{export_id}/download issues a short-lived signed URL for the CSV object. (2) Export worker: background job builds the CSV from the same aggregation dataset used by the interactive breakdown table, with consistent rounding/currency and stable column order. (3) Signed download URLs: objects stored under a workspace-scoped prefix; URL TTL defaults to 15 minutes; server returns a URL only after RBAC checks. (4) Audit events: emits usage_insights.export_created, usage_insights.export_download_requested, usage_insights.export_downloaded (when we can observe it), and usage_insights.export_failed with actor/workspace/export_id, filters hash, row_count, bytes, and client metadata (ip/user_agent). (5) Guardrails/limits: max time range 31 days, max 200k rows, max 250MB output, and per-workspace export concurrency limit (default 2). Adds rate limiting on create + download issue endpoints to prevent abuse and reduce object-store churn.

API details: - Request supports the same filters as breakdown queries (workspace/project, time range, route, model family/version bucket, deployment mode, region). - Exports are immutable: filters are stored on create and used by worker; the GET endpoint returns the canonical filters hash for reconciliation and support debugging. - Pagination is not exposed for CSV export; instead we enforce server-side limits and return a clear error code/message when exceeded.

Security/RBAC: Export creation and download issuance require the same permission gate as cost views (CostViewer or WorkspaceAdmin, plus workspace setting allow_cost_export=true). For restricted workspaces, export is hard-disabled and returns 403 with a dedicated error code. Signed URLs are generated server-side only; no client-provided object keys. Objects are encrypted at rest and scoped by workspace prefix; worker uses a service role with write-only permissions to that prefix.

Operational notes: - Worker retries are capped (3 attempts) and marked failed with an error code suitable for console display. - Adds metrics: export_job_runtime_ms, export_job_bytes, export_rows, export_failures_total, and signed_url_issues_total. - Adds log fields: export_id, workspace_id, actor_id, filters_hash.

Test plan: - Unit tests for request validation, limit enforcement, and column mapping. - Integration tests for job lifecycle (queued -> running -> complete -> download URL issued) using fake object store. - Security tests for RBAC gating (cost hidden, restricted workspace, non-member). - Manual: console trigger export for 24h/7d, verify totals match breakdown view within rounding; verify signed URL expires; verify audit events appear in audit log stream.

Notes/Follow-ups: - We cannot reliably emit export_downloaded for all clients (signed URL is direct to object store). We currently emit export_download_requested when issuing the URL; export_downloaded is best-effort based on object-store access logs where available. - Scheduled exports/email is out of scope.

Screenshots: n/a (backend only).

Checklist: - [x] Limits documented in endpoint comments - [x] Added audit events and validated schema with Security - [x] Added rate limiting - [x] Added integration tests - [x] Verified export matches billing totals for sample fixtures (see tests in reconciliation helpers)

title: RBAC gating for cost views + CSV export; add audit log events
preview:
Summary: Enforces RBAC for Usage Insights cost views and export flows, adds a workspace-level setting to hide cost data, and emits audit log events for export generation/download and share-link access. This is a prerequisite for enabling the Usage Insights beta for enterprise workspaces with stricter governance.

Context / motivation: Usage Insights introduces cost/usage breakdowns by route+model and a CSV export path. Security review flagged two gaps: (1) existing "usage" permissions were sufficient to view token counts but not appropriate for cost visibility, and (2) export/download actions were not consistently audited. We also need a workspace admin control to disable cost visibility in restricted workspaces while still allowing non-cost usage metrics.

What changed:
- RBAC: Introduced a dedicated permission gate for cost visibility and export actions.
  - New permission: cost.read (for cost columns/fields, totals, currency display).
  - New permission: cost.export (for creating export jobs, retrieving signed URLs).
  - Existing usage.read continues to allow non-cost usage metrics where applicable.
- Workspace setting: Added workspace_settings.hide_cost (default false).
  - If hide_cost=true, server returns cost fields as null/omitted and the UI should render "Cost hidden by workspace admin" messaging (UI wiring is in a separate PR).
  - hide_cost does not block non-cost usage views (tokens, request counts), but does block exports that include cost columns.
- Endpoint enforcement:
  - usage-insights query endpoints now require usage.read.
  - Any response including monetary cost requires cost.read AND hide_cost=false.
  - Export endpoints require cost.export AND hide_cost=false.
- Audit logging:
  - Added audit events:
    - usage_insights.export_job_created
    - usage_insights.export_downloaded
    - usage_insights.share_link_viewed (when report links are accessed)
  - Audit payload includes actor_id, workspace_id, project_id (when present), time_range, filters (route/model), export_format, row_count (when available), and client_ip/user_agent.
  - Added fields requested in INT-9140 (download URL redaction + export_job_id correlation).
- Error handling / semantics:
  - 403 for missing permissions (with explicit "required_permission" in error metadata).
  - 412 when workspace setting hide_cost=true and caller requests cost data/export.
  - Ensures consistent behavior across REST and internal RPC adapters.

Security review notes (Marcus/Kevin):
- We intentionally do NOT log raw query strings or prompt content in audit payloads.
- Filter values are constrained to normalized route templates and model identifiers (already normalized/bucketed upstream).
- Signed download URLs are never stored in audit logs; we log export_job_id and a short-lived download token hash.
- Retention: audit events follow existing audit log retention policy; export artifacts follow export retention policy (handled in export service).

Testing:
- Unit tests for RBAC evaluator (permission matrix across roles: Workspace Admin, Billing Admin, Developer, Viewer).
- Integration tests for /usage-insights/* endpoints verifying 403/412 behaviors and field redaction.
- Added golden tests for audit event payload shape (ensures INT-9140 fields stay present).

Rollout notes:
- This is gated behind the existing feature flag for Usage Insights beta; enforcement is server-side and safe to enable before UI changes.
- Follow-up: Console UI will hide cost columns and disable Export CTA based on new "cost_visibility" capability field (tracked in ENG-3820 subtask).

Screenshots: N/A (backend-only).

Risk / mitigations:
- Risk: Some customers rely on existing "usage" roles to see cost in older billing views. Mitigation: cost.read is only applied to Usage Insights endpoints; existing billing pages are unchanged.
- Risk: Export clients may see new 412 errors when hide_cost is enabled. Mitigation: error message includes actionable guidance (admin setting + required permission).

title: Add Usage Insights freshness/quality dashboard + alerts (lag + reconciliation drift)
preview:
Motivation: Usage Insights is going into beta with interactive console queries backed by hourly/day partitions and reconciliation checks against billing. We need a standard dashboard + alert bundle in observability-pack so SRE/on-call can quickly answer: (1) is data fresh, (2) are we dropping/late-ingesting events by route/model, (3) are we drifting vs billing totals beyond tolerance, and (4) are query endpoints timing out for common ranges.

This PR adds a Grafana dashboard and alert rule templates for freshness lag, late event rate, reconciliation drift, and query p95 latency. The dashboard is designed to be imported into customer-facing/internal stacks with minimal per-cluster edits (templated variables for env/region/deployment_mode/workspace_id).

What’s included: 1) Dashboard: “Usage Insights — Data Freshness & Quality” (Grafana JSON) Panels: Aggregation lag (p50/p95) by dataset (route_model_hourly, route_model_daily) and by region; Ingestion late-event ratio (late_events / total_events) by region + streaming endpoint flag; Missing route_tag rate (events where route_template = "unknown" or null); Reconciliation drift (%) between insights totals and billing hook totals, per workspace and overall; Top offenders table: workspaces with highest drift and highest lag; Query API p95 latency + error rate (v1/usage-insights/* endpoints) with annotations for deploys.

2) Alerts (PrometheusRule templates): - UsageInsightsAggregationLagHigh: fires when p95 aggregation lag > 20m for 15m (warn) and > 60m for 10m (page). - UsageInsightsLateEventRatioHigh: fires when late_event_ratio > 2% for 30m (warn), with a separate page threshold at 5% for 15m. - UsageInsightsRouteTagMissing: fires when missing_route_tag_rate > 1% for 30m (warn) and > 3% for 15m (page). - UsageInsightsReconciliationDriftHigh: fires when abs(drift_pct) > 0.5% for 2h (warn) and > 1.0% for 1h (page), excluding small totals (< $50/day) to reduce noise. - UsageInsightsQueryP95Degraded: fires when p95 > 2500ms for 15m (warn) and > 5000ms for 10m (page), scoped to the insights query service.

3) Runbook stub: Adds a runbook page with investigation steps and links to relevant services/datasets (warehouse partitions, ingestion pipeline, billing hook job).

Metric assumptions (coordinated with Telemetry/Analytics): This PR uses the metric names already in-flight on redwood (see ENG-3824/ENG-3817): usage_insights_aggregation_lag_seconds{dataset,region,deployment_mode}; usage_insights_late_event_ratio{region,streaming}; usage_insights_missing_route_tag_rate{region}; usage_insights_reconciliation_drift_pct{workspace_id}; usage_insights_query_request_duration_ms_bucket{route,status}; usage_insights_query_errors_total{route,status}. If any naming changes land before beta, we can follow up with a patch release of observability-pack.

Noise/guardrails: The drift alert includes a minimum spend floor and a 2h lookback to avoid paging on transient rounding/timing skew (e.g., hourly partitions catching up). The lag alert pages only on sustained lag > 60m.

Testing/validation: Imported dashboard into staging Grafana; validated queries on canary metrics in us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Used replayed lag/drift fixtures from staging to verify alert routing and labels.

Rollout notes: This is safe to ship ahead of beta. SREs should import the dashboard to the internal "Console & Tooling" folder and ensure Alertmanager routes page severity=page to #eng-sre, and warn severity=warn to #eng-platform.

Screenshots: (not included in PR; Grafana JSON is the source of truth).

title: Audit logs for shadow experiment + shadow policy changes
preview:
Summary: Adds audit log entries for shadow experiment lifecycle actions (create/update/archive) and for route-attached shadow policy updates. This is part of Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics beta readiness: we need a clear who/what/when trail for experiment changes and policy toggles, especially for Dedicated/Private tenants with compliance requirements.

Context / Motivation: Shadow traffic is explicitly “fail-open” and non-user-impacting, but misconfiguration can still create meaningful cost/retention risk (sampling rate, targets, redaction flags). During dogfood and early customer discussions, we’ve been asked to provide auditable records for: (1) who created/changed an experiment, (2) who attached/detached shadow policies to routes/rollouts, and (3) what fields changed. This PR extends the existing AuditLog service to capture the relevant actions and adds minimal schema support for structured diffs.

What’s included: (1) New audit event types: SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_CREATED, SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_UPDATED, SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_ARCHIVED, SHADOW_POLICY_ATTACHED, SHADOW_POLICY_UPDATED, SHADOW_POLICY_DETACHED. (2) Console backend wiring: emits audit events from ShadowExperiments CRUD endpoints and from policy mutation endpoints used by rollouts. (3) Schema updates: adds resource_type = "shadow_experiment" | "shadow_policy" and resource_id fields, plus optional change_summary (field-level diff) with allowlisted keys to avoid leaking prompt/content. (4) RBAC + attribution: ensures actor is the authenticated user/service principal; includes org_id + project_id + environment in the audit payload for correlation in Private deployments. (5) Tests: unit tests for event emission and redaction/allowlist behavior; integration test verifies a mutation request produces a single audit row with stable event_type.

Not included / Follow-ups: No UI changes in Console for viewing these events (tracked separately). No backfill for historic changes prior to this PR. We intentionally do not log prompt content, model outputs, or header values; only configuration metadata and allowlisted field diffs.

Implementation notes: Audit payload fields are explicitly allowlisted. For SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_UPDATED, we capture changes for: name, description, sampling_rate, target_variants, rubric_bundle_id, and retention_mode. For shadow policy events, we capture: enabled, sampling_rate, shadow_targets (IDs only), fail_open, and timeout_ms. Anything else is recorded as "[redacted]" in change_summary.

Risk: Low. Audit emission is best-effort and does not block the write path (fail-open). If AuditLog is down/slow, we enqueue to the existing async audit buffer with bounded retries.

Validation: Ran console-backend unit tests + integration suite, and verified in staging that create/update/archive actions produce expected event types with correct resource_id. Confirmed no request body or prompt fields end up in the audit record.

Screenshots/Logs: Included in internal staging run (see test output in CI artifacts).

Release note (internal): Shadow experiments and shadow policies now generate audit log events on create/update/archive/attach/detach.
