During dogfood and early customer discussions, we’ve been asked to provide auditable records for: (1) who created/changed an experiment, (2) who attached/detached shadow policies to routes/rollouts, and (3) what fields changed. This PR extends the existing AuditLog service to capture the relevant actions and adds minimal schema support for structured diffs.

What’s included: (1) New audit event types: SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_CREATED, SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_UPDATED, SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_ARCHIVED, SHADOW_POLICY_ATTACHED, SHADOW_POLICY_UPDATED, SHADOW_POLICY_DETACHED. (2) Console backend wiring: emits audit events from ShadowExperiments CRUD endpoints and from policy mutation endpoints used by rollouts. (3) Schema updates: adds resource_type = "shadow_experiment" | "shadow_policy" and resource_id fields, plus optional change_summary (field-level diff) with allowlisted keys to avoid leaking prompt/content. (4) RBAC + attribution: ensures actor is the authenticated user/service principal; includes org_id + project_id + environment in the audit payload for correlation in Private deployments. (5) Tests: unit tests for event emission and redaction/allowlist behavior; integration test verifies a mutation request produces a single audit row with stable event_type.

Not included / Follow-ups: No UI changes in Console for viewing these events (tracked separately). No backfill for historic changes prior to this PR. We intentionally do not log prompt content, model outputs, or header values; only configuration metadata and allowlisted field diffs.

Implementation notes: Audit payload fields are explicitly allowlisted. For SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_UPDATED, we capture changes for: name, description, sampling_rate, target_variants, rubric_bundle_id, and retention_mode. For shadow policy events, we capture: enabled, sampling_rate, shadow_targets (IDs only), fail_open, and timeout_ms. Anything else is recorded as "[redacted]" in change_summary.

Risk: Low. Audit emission is best-effort and does not block the write path (fail-open). If AuditLog is down/slow, we enqueue to the existing async audit buffer with bounded retries.

Validation: Ran console-backend unit tests + integration suite, and verified in staging that create/update/archive actions produce expected event types with correct resource_id. Confirmed no request body or prompt fields end up in the audit record.

Screenshots/Logs: Included in internal staging run (see test output in CI artifacts).

Release note (internal): Shadow experiments and shadow policies now generate audit log events on create/update/archive/attach/detach.

title: Console API: shadow experiments CRUD + aggregates endpoints (RBAC, pagination)
preview: Summary: Implements Console backend endpoints to create/update/query Shadow Experiments and fetch quality/latency/cost aggregates for Console drilldowns. This is the first public-facing server API for shadow experiments; UI work will follow behind a feature flag. Motivation: Beta customers requested a safe way to attach quality signals to rollouts without impacting user traffic; this PR unblocks dogfood and Dedicated beta by providing stable CRUD + query primitives with strict RBAC and cardinality-safe filters. API surface (v1, behind feature flag `shadow_experiments_api`): POST /v1/shadow-experiments (create), GET /v1/shadow-experiments (list w/ cursor pagination), GET /v1/shadow-experiments/:id (details), PATCH /v1/shadow-experiments/:id (mutate mutable fields only), POST /v1/shadow-experiments/:id:archive (soft-archive), GET /v1/shadow-experiments/:id/aggregates (time-windowed metric deltas + confidence intervals stub), GET /v1/shadow-experiments/:id/examples (sampled paired examples; gated + capped). RBAC: Requires `rollouts:write` for create/mutate/archive; `rollouts:read` for list/get/aggregates. Access is tenant-scoped; Dedicated sub-org scoping enforced via `org_id` + `project_id`. Additionally, `examples` endpoint requires `quality:read_sensitive` to reduce accidental prompt/response exposure. Pagination/filtering: List uses opaque cursor (base64) with stable ordering by created_at,id. Filters supported: route_id, rollout_id, state (active|archived), created_after/before, tag (low-cardinality). Hard caps: limit <= 200; default 50. Aggregates endpoint supports fixed bucket sizes (5m/1h/1d) and clamps time range to retention window; rejects arbitrary group-by dimensions to avoid metric blowups. Data model: Adds `shadow_experiments` and `shadow_experiment_targets` tables (one control + N shadow targets) and stores only non-sensitive config references (route_id, rollout_id, policy_ref, rubric_version, sampling_rate, metadata allowlist). No prompt/response payload storage is added here (handled by telemetry pipeline); `examples` endpoint reads from the shadow artifacts service via signed references and returns redacted fields only. Validation: Enforces sampling_rate bounds, target uniqueness, and immutability rules (cannot change control target or bucketing salt after activation). Also validates that referenced route is eligible for shadowing (streaming behavior allowed but marked `partial_capture=true`). Observability: Adds request logs + metrics for endpoint QPS/latency and a counter for RBAC denials; aggregates endpoint emits `shadow_console_api_aggregate_requests_total{status}`. Testing: Unit tests for validators + pagination cursor codec; integration tests for RBAC and tenancy boundaries; contract-style tests for list filters; added a small seed helper for local dev. Notes/Follow-ups: (1) Aggregate confidence intervals currently return placeholder fields when the scoring pipeline hasn’t produced CI; Console will render “insufficient data” until ENG-5405/ENG-5406 complete. (2) Audit log entries for create/update/archive are wired, but the expanded audit event schema will land in a dedicated PR (ENG-5417).

title: Console: marketplace onboarding entrypoint + improved entitlement error handling
preview: Summary: Adds a marketplace-specific entrypoint to the Console onboarding flow (AWS/GCP) with clearer states for entitlement linking and improved error handling/retry guidance. Motivation: Marketplace buyers are currently landing in the generic signup flow and getting stuck on entitlement propagation and “already has org” edge cases; this PR adds a dedicated path and maps entitlement verification errors to actionable UX. Scope: (1) New /onboarding/marketplace route and “Continue from AWS/GCP Marketplace” CTA on the signup screen behind feature flag console.marketplaceOnboardingEntrypoint. (2) Marketplace-specific stepper screens: Connect subscription → Link or create Redwood org → Create first API key → Next steps. (3) Error mapping for entitlement verification endpoint responses (from PR #19875) with user-facing copy, retry timers, and support handoff. (4) Edge-case handling: existing orgs (prompt to link), multiple subscriptions per org (select subscription), delayed entitlement propagation (retry + status), and “wrong cloud” subscriptions (AWS vs GCP mismatch). (5) Adds instrumentation hooks for funnel stages (events used by ENG-22114/PR #19877), but does not ship dashboards here. UX notes: Includes design review changes requested in DES-912 (copy, button labels, and layout adjustments) and a persistent “Need help?” panel linking to marketplace onboarding docs + support. Testing: Added unit tests for error mapping + integration-ish tests for the stepper transitions; manual QA performed in staging with mocked entitlement responses. Rollout: Feature flag default OFF; enable in staging immediately; production ramp planned after entitlement sync job hardening (PR #19878).

title: Shadow traffic: retention defaults + prompt/response redaction pipeline
preview: Context: Shadow Traffic generates additional artifacts (request/response pairs + scoring payload references). We need strict data minimization by default and deterministic redaction so we can store enough to compute quality deltas while meeting Dedicated/Private constraints. This PR implements (1) retention defaults by deployment mode, (2) a redaction/transform pipeline for shadow artifacts, and (3) configuration toggles + audit-friendly metadata to support regulated customers. Summary of changes: - Adds `shadow_artifact_retention` config with safe defaults (Hosted: 24h; Dedicated: 7d; Private: 0h unless explicitly configured) and per-tenant overrides with validation. - Introduces `ShadowArtifactRedactor` (server-side) that transforms prompts/inputs and outputs before persistence: structured allowlist, truncation, hashing of identifiers, and regex-based PII scrubbing. - Adds storage layout for artifacts with TTL enforcement (DB TTL where supported + object-store lifecycle rules) and a periodic GC job for backends without native TTL. - Adds knobs for Dedicated/Private: `retention_hours`, `store_raw_prompts` (default false), `store_raw_responses` (default false), `redaction_profile` (standard|strict), and `export_audit_metadata` for SIEM export. - Updates ingestion to persist only redacted payloads; raw payloads never leave the request handler boundary (fail-closed for raw capture, fail-open for shadowing overall). - Adds metrics + logs for redaction effectiveness and retention GC: `shadow.redaction.actions_total`, `shadow.redaction.bytes_dropped`, `shadow.retention.gc.deleted_total`, and `shadow.retention.gc.lag_seconds`. - Docs in code comments reference Confluence: Security & Compliance > Shadow traffic data retention and redaction (v1). Behavior details: - Redaction is applied prior to enqueueing scoring payload references; scoring workers fetch only redacted text blobs. - Prompt redaction uses a best-effort regex pass (email/phone/SSN-like patterns) plus an allowlist for structured inputs (`messages[].role`, `messages[].content`); everything else is dropped unless explicitly allowed. - Content truncation defaults: prompts 8KB, responses 16KB (post-redaction), configurable only for Dedicated/Private with max caps enforced. - Stable hashing uses HMAC-SHA256 with a per-tenant key (KMS-managed) for `user_id`, `session_id`, and request fingerprints so customers can correlate within their tenant without exposing raw values. - Retention is enforced on both the artifact table and associated blob objects; deletion is idempotent and safe to re-run. Risk/mitigations: - Cardinality: we do not emit raw identifiers in metrics; only redaction action counts and coarse-grained profiles. - Performance: redaction runs in the async persistence path; synchronous request path only copies required fields into an internal buffer and immediately drops raw references after redaction. - Private deployments: when object-store lifecycle rules are unavailable, the GC job is mandatory and will run as part of the telemetry stack; documented in release notes for Private packaging. Testing: - Unit tests for allowlist behavior, regex scrubber, truncation, and hashing determinism. - Integration test that verifies raw prompts are not persisted when `store_raw_prompts=false`. - Migration test for existing shadow artifact rows (backfill redaction on read is NOT supported; this PR gates storage behind the feature flag so we don’t create mixed-mode tables). Rollout: gated behind `shadow_artifacts_persist_redacted_v1` (default off). Dedicated/Private can override via control plane config. Follow-ups: - Implement customer-configurable field-level allowlists for JSON tool inputs (deferred; requires product + security review). - Add UI surfacing of retention profile in Console (tracked in ENG-5408).

title: Add schema_version tagging, JSON Schema validation, and legacy event normalization to v1
preview:
## Summary
This PR updates the audit-log-exporter to:
- Tag all outgoing events with `schema_version` (default: `v1.0`).
- Validate outgoing payloads against the canonical Audit Log Schema v1 (JSON Schema) and emit exporter-side metrics when validation fails.
- Normalize legacy/heterogeneous producer payloads into the v1 canonical shape to eliminate field drift across event types.

This is the first step to making SIEM exports consistent across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments and to support SOC2/ISO evidence expectations (stable schema + documented backcompat behavior).

## Motivation
We have multiple event producers emitting “almost the same” semantics but with different field names and shapes. Customers exporting to Splunk/Sentinel/Datadog see inconsistent keys across event types (SUP-18477). Additionally, exporter retries can cause duplicates (SUP-18510); while idempotency is ultimately a downstream concern, consistent `event_id` + stable schema helps customers dedupe.

## What changed
1) schema_version tagging
- Add `schema_version` to every exported event.
- If an incoming event already includes `schema_version`, we preserve it (but still normalize/validate).
- If missing, exporter sets `schema_version` to `v1.0` after normalization.

2) JSON Schema validation (export-time)
- Added a `validator` module that loads the Audit Log Schema v1 JSON Schema artifact.
- Validation happens after normalization and before delivery to sinks.
- On validation failure:
  - Increment `audit_exporter_event_validation_failed_total{reason=...}`
  - Emit a structured log with `event_id`, `event_type`, `tenant_id`, and `validation_error_summary`
  - Continue delivery behavior is configurable:
    - Default: "best-effort" delivery (do not drop), but attach `exporter_warnings=["schema_validation_failed"]` for downstream visibility.
    - Optional: strict mode (drop invalid events) for internal testing environments.

3) Normalization (legacy -> v1 canonical)
- Introduced a canonical mapping layer that transforms known legacy fields into v1 fields:
  - `account_id` -> `tenant_id`
  - `org_id` -> `tenant_id` (only when `account_id` absent; logs warning if both present and disagree)
  - `user` / `user_email` / `actor_email` -> `actor.display_name` or `actor.email` (only if present; avoid synthesizing PII)
  - `ip` / `sourceIp` -> `source.ip`
  - `requestId` / `x_request_id` -> `request_id`
  - `traceId` -> `trace_id`
  - `type` / `action` -> `event_type` (mapped via allowlist registry)
  - `timestamp` (seconds) / `ts` -> `occurred_at` (RFC3339)
- Adds a deterministic `event_id` if missing (ULID) and preserves producer-provided `event_id` if present.
- Adds a stable `export_id` (UUID) per export attempt for debugging exporter retries.

4) Metrics + observability
- Added metrics:
  - `audit_exporter_events_normalized_total{schema_version=..., producer_hint=...}`
  - `audit_exporter_event_validation_failed_total{reason=...}`
  - `audit_exporter_event_schema_version_missing_total`
- Added debug-level logs for normalization decisions guarded by sampling (to avoid log spam).

## Example payload (after normalization)
Example shown here is illustrative; actual payload is flattened per existing exporter conventions, with a stable set of top-level keys. After normalization + tagging:
{"schema_version":"v1.0","event_id":"01J3N7KQKQ3QH7JQKJ0Z3V8M2M","event_type":"rbac.role.updated","occurred_at":"2026-02-04T18:22:11.219Z","tenant_id":"tnt_8f3c...","actor_type":"user","actor_id":"usr_2a91...","actor_email_hash":"sha256:...","source_ip":"203.0.113.10","request_id":"req_1f6d...","trace_id":"1-65b...","target_type":"role","target_id":"role_admin","action_result":"success"}

Notes:
- Email is not exported as raw PII; exporter hashes to `actor_email_hash` when configured and when input contains email. This aligns with ongoing redaction guidelines.
- `schema_version` is always present.

## Configuration changes
- `export.validation.mode`: `best_effort` (default) | `strict`
- `export.normalization.enabled`: true (default)
- `export.normalization.allow_legacy_event_types`: false (default). When true, legacy `type/action` strings can pass through if unmapped, but are tagged with `exporter_warnings=["unknown_event_type"]`.

## Testing
- Unit tests: normalization mapping cases (account/org id precedence, timestamp parsing, request/trace propagation, event_type mapping).
- Unit tests: JSON Schema validation pass/fail cases.
- Integration-ish tests: sink payload contains `schema_version` and stable required fields.

## Rollout plan
- Ship behind config toggles already supported by Helm values in Private.
- Start with Hosted exporter in best-effort mode, monitor validation failure metric.
- After one week with low failure rate, enable strict mode in CI and staging only.

## Risk/compat
- This PR is intentionally non-breaking for customers consuming exported events: it only adds `schema_version` and stabilizes field names.
- Some customers may have built dashboards keyed off legacy fields; normalization keeps the legacy fields out of the exported payload by default to reduce drift. If we see breakage, we can add a temporary `export.include_legacy_fields=true` compatibility switch (not included in this PR to avoid schema bloat).

## Links
- Linear: ENG-4827 (Exporter normalization + schema version tagging)
- Related: ENG-4833 (Legacy producer migration plan)
- Support: SUP-18477 (inconsistent fields), SUP-18510 (duplicates due to retries)

---
Checklist
- [x] Add schema_version tagging
- [x] Validate against schema v1 JSON Schema
- [x] Normalize known legacy producers
- [x] Add metrics + dashboards hooks
- [x] Document config toggles in exporter README

title: Policy engine: emit v1 audit events for routing policy + fallback updates (diff + cardinality checks)
preview:
Context
We’re standardizing audit logging coverage for sensitive actions across Hosted/Dedicated/Private. Routing policy changes (incl. fallback rules) are security-relevant because they can alter what models serve which requests, potentially impacting data handling boundaries and reliability controls.

This PR instruments the policy engine to emit schema v1 audit events for routing policy CRUD + publish operations, with:
- A bounded diff generator (before/after) to support investigations
- Cardinality guards to prevent high-cardinality payloads from polluting downstream SIEMs
- Consistent actor/target semantics + correlation IDs per the v1 standard

What’s included
- Audit events emitted for:
  - Routing policy create/update/delete
  - Fallback configuration update (primary/fallback model sets, failure-mode rules)
  - Allow/deny list changes for model routing
  - Policy publish/activate (when a draft becomes effective)
- New event types (registered):
  - policy.routing.created
  - policy.routing.updated
  - policy.routing.deleted
  - policy.routing.published
  - policy.fallback.updated
- Diff summary generation:
  - Produces a stable, bounded summary string + structured keys for a small allow-list of fields
  - Drops/obfuscates unbounded data (e.g., free-form match expressions) and emits a reason tag (diff_truncated=true, truncated_fields=[...])
  - For lists (e.g., model allow-lists), emits added/removed counts and a capped sample (max 10) of normalized IDs
- Cardinality checks:
  - Enforces allow-listing for attribute keys on the audit event extension payload (policy.* namespace)
  - Normalizes/caps values: strings max 256 chars, lists max 50 entries, emits *_count fields for full sizes
  - Rejects emission only for the extension payload (event still emitted with minimal required fields) and logs an internal metric audit_extension_dropped_total{reason=...}
- Correlation + attribution:
  - request_id + trace_id propagated from gateway -> policy service
  - actor derived from auth context (user/service principal); supports impersonation context when present
  - source_ip captured when available (gateway forwarded header), otherwise omitted (not synthesized)

Files / components touched (high level)
- services/policy-engine
  - routing_policy_service.go: emit events on write paths
  - diff/routing_policy_diff.go: allow-listed diff generation + truncation
  - audit/policy_audit_emitter.go: helper to build v1 events + extension validation
- libs/audit
  - event_types_registry.ts: register new event types + required fields
  - cardinality/guards.ts: namespace allow-list + value caps
- tests
  - unit tests for diff (order-insensitive list behavior, truncation, cap enforcement)
  - integration test for routing policy update emitting policy.routing.updated with expected fields

Example emitted event (sanitized)
- event_type: policy.routing.updated
- action: update
- actor_type: user
- actor_id: usr_01HT…
- resource_type: routing_policy
- resource_id: rp_7f3a…
- scope: tenant
- schema_version: 1.0
- extension.policy.diff_summary: "fallback_mode: 'region' -> 'model_variant'; allow_list_models: +2/-1 (sample: [mdl_llama3_70b_q4, mdl_llama3_70b_fp16])"
- extension.policy.diff_truncated: false

Design notes / trade-offs
- We intentionally avoid embedding raw match expressions or full JSON policy documents in audit logs (PII risk + cardinality). Instead we emit:
  - A bounded diff summary
  - Counts + small samples of normalized IDs
  - A pointer to the policy_id/version to fetch the full policy from the control plane if the investigator has authorization
- Event delivery is best-effort for this service (aligned with ADR in progress), but we emit metrics on failures and do not block policy writes on audit sink issues.

Testing
- go test ./services/policy-engine/... (new diff + emitter unit tests)
- Integration: TestRoutingPolicyUpdateEmitsAuditEvent (uses audit test sink)
- Lint: event_type registry enforcement (fails build on unregistered types)

Rollout
- Behind feature flag: AUDIT_V1_POLICY_ENGINE_EVENTS (default on in staging, can be enabled per-environment)
- Observability: new metrics
  - audit_events_emitted_total{event_type=...}
  - audit_events_failed_total{reason=...}
  - audit_extension_dropped_total{reason=...}

Links
- Linear: ENG-4838 (policy engine audit events)
- Related: SUP-18477 (customer reports inconsistent fields in SIEM exports)

Review notes
- Please focus on: diff allow-list correctness, cardinality caps, and whether the event types/fields match the taxonomy we’re converging on.

---
Conversation / review timeline
2026-01-29 (Allison Grant): "Diff logic looks good, but please ensure truncation is explicit to the consumer. Also the sample list must be deterministic (sort) to avoid noisy SIEM diffs." 
2026-01-29 (Jordan Lee): "Ack. Added diff_truncated + truncated_fields and made model ID sampling sorted after normalization. Updated tests for determinism."

2026-01-30 (Ethan Park): "We should include policy_version in the event so investigators can correlate which revision was applied. Also: do we emit for publish vs update?" 
2026-01-30 (Jordan Lee): "Added resource_version (policy_version) and a separate policy.routing.published event emitted only on activate. Update continues to fire for draft edits." 

2026-02-01 (Marcus Lin): "Cardinality guard behavior: do not drop the entire event if extension fails validation; keep minimal event and record a metric. Also check source_ip handling (don’t trust arbitrary headers)." 
2026-02-01 (Jordan Lee): "Implemented ‘extension drop’ path: event always emitted with required fields; extension omitted on validation error; metric increments with reason. source_ip now only populated from gateway-provided verified context (not raw header) with fallback to empty." 

2026-02-03 (Logan Wright): "Can we add a small integration test assertion for correlation IDs? We’ve had gaps in request_id propagation." 
2026-02-03 (Jordan Lee): "Added assertion for request_id + trace_id in integration test when the test harness includes a trace context." 

CI
- Initial run failed (2026-02-02): lint rejected unregistered event type policy.fallback.updated (registry update was in separate commit) 
- Rerun after registry fix: all checks green

Final approvals
- Allison Grant: Approved (2026-02-04)
- Ethan Park: Approved (2026-02-04)
- Marcus Lin: Approved (2026-02-04)

Merge
Squash merged to main on 2026-02-04. Flag enabled in staging; production rollout tracked in ENG-4838 checklist.

title: Shadow traffic: telemetry events + ingestion hooks (v1)
preview:
Context: Shadow traffic routing (ENG-5402) mirrors a sampled subset of prod requests to one or more shadow targets. To compute quality deltas (and to debug routing/scoring lag) we need a low-cardinality, privacy-aware set of telemetry events that (a) links the control and shadow inference outcomes, (b) can be ingested reliably in Hosted/Dedicated/Private, and (c) provides enough dimensions to slice by route/model/region without exploding metrics cardinality.

This PR introduces shadow traffic telemetry events and wires them into the ingestion pipeline (Kafka -> ingest -> warehouse tables) with initial dashboard stubs for internal dogfood.

What’s included: (1) New event schemas: shadow_pair_created (emitted when the mirror is scheduled), shadow_inference_completed (shadow target returned or timed out), and shadow_scoring_status (async scoring queued/completed/failed). Each event includes a stable shadow_pair_id and request_fingerprint (HMAC), plus required dimensions: tenant_id (hashed), route_id (low-card), control_model_id, shadow_target_id, deployment_mode, region, outcome, and timing fields. (2) Cardinality controls: disallow freeform tags in shadow telemetry, bucket user_agent, truncate prompt_length/response_length into fixed buckets, and explicitly drop prompt_id / conversation_id at emit-time. Route-level and model-level dimensions are included; anything higher-card must be attached via example sampling endpoints later (out of scope). (3) Ingestion pipeline hooks: adds validators/normalizers for the new events, forwards them through the existing ingest service, and creates the initial storage tables shadow_pairs_v1 and shadow_scoring_v1 (partitioned by day, clustered by tenant_hash + route_id). (4) Initial dashboards stubs: adds basic Grafana JSON stubs for shadow throughput, shadow target error rate, and scoring lag; these are intentionally minimal and will be expanded in observability-pack PR-391.

PII / data handling: This PR does not store raw prompts/responses. request_fingerprint is computed using an HMAC keyed per environment (see config), and tenant_id is hashed before leaving the data plane. Any headers included are allowlisted (content-type, accept, x-redwood-route) and explicitly exclude auth/cookie-like fields.

Testing: Unit tests for schema validation and normalization, golden tests for event-to-table mapping, and an end-to-end ingest test that emits a synthetic shadow_pair_created + shadow_inference_completed and asserts the warehouse row shape. Load testing / overhead benchmarking is tracked in ENG-5412.

Rollout plan: gated behind feature flag shadow_telemetry_v1 (default off). Dogfood tenants can enable via internal config. Fail-open: telemetry emission failures do not impact request path; ingestion lag only affects dashboards/metrics.

Follow-ups: (a) scoring payload references once redaction pipeline lands (ENG-5411), (b) additional aggregation views for confidence intervals, (c) tighten SLO alert thresholds once we have baseline dogfood data.

Notes for reviewers: please focus on (1) schema field choices and defaults, (2) cardinality guardrails, and (3) whether the ingestion mapping matches the query patterns we expect for Console drilldowns.

title: Control plane: emit v1 audit events for RBAC CRUD endpoints
preview: Context: We’re standardizing audit logging across Hosted/Dedicated/Private with a canonical v1 schema. RBAC is a top-tier sensitive surface (role definitions, role bindings, membership changes), and we’ve had customer reports of missing RBAC change events in Private (SUP-18421). This PR instruments the control plane RBAC service (and the HTTP layer that fronts it) to emit v1 audit events for RBAC CRUD actions with consistent actor attribution, safe diff summaries, and correlation IDs (request_id/trace_id/admin_action_id) for investigations. What’s included: (1) v1 audit event emission for RBAC endpoints: role.create, role.update, role.delete; role_binding.create/update/delete; membership add/remove; project/tenant-scoped permission bundle updates. (2) Actor identity + impersonation support: actor.type (user|service_account|service), actor.id, actor.email (when available), actor.session_id, actor.impersonated_by.* (when admin impersonation is used), and source_ip/user_agent pulled from request context. (3) Diff summaries for update operations: we generate a constrained JSON Patch-style diff (ops limited + size capped) and also include a human-friendly summary string for common RBAC changes (“added permission: models.use”, “removed member: user:abc”). (4) Correlation: request_id + trace_id propagated from gateway headers, plus admin_action_id generated for Console-initiated workflows and included in both the HTTP response and emitted audit events. (5) Safety + operability: audit emission is best-effort (does not block RBAC writes on sink failure) but increments metrics and logs structured errors; payload fields are classified and redacted (no secrets; free-form strings truncated; role descriptions not included in diffs). Testing: unit tests for diff generator + actor extraction; integration tests that exercise RBAC endpoints and assert a schema-valid v1 audit event is emitted via the in-memory audit sink. Example emitted event (redacted): {"schema_version":"1.0","event_type":"rbac.role.update","event_id":"01HRQ0...","occurred_at":"2026-02-03T19:22:11.482Z","tenant_id":"tnt_9f3...","project_id":"prj_12a...","actor.type":"user","actor.id":"usr_3c1...","actor.email":"admin@customer.com","actor.ip":"203.0.113.10","actor.impersonated_by.id":"usr_8aa...","request_id":"req_7b1...","trace_id":"9b2f...","admin_action_id":"admact_17c...","target.type":"rbac.role","target.id":"role_29d...","change.summary":"added permission: routing.policy.write","change.diff":"[{\"op\":\"add\",\"path\":\"/permissions/4\",\"value\":\"routing.policy.write\"}]"}. Rollout notes: guarded behind feature flag AUDIT_V1_RBAC_ENABLED (default on in staging, off in prod until exporter normalization is deployed); legacy audit events (pre-v1) are left unchanged for now (ENG-4833 handles normalization/deprecation). Follow-ups: Console needs to pass admin_action_id consistently for all RBAC admin flows; Private parity tracked in the installer/exporter workstreams. Checklist: - [x] Event types registered in taxonomy registry - [x] Required fields present per schema v1 - [x] No PII/secrets logged; diff capped and validated - [x] Metrics for emit failures added - [x] Integration tests added and running in CI

title: Audit events: API key create/rotate/revoke (v1 schema + redaction)
preview: Summary This PR adds canonical v1 audit events for API key lifecycle actions in the control plane: creation, rotation (new key issued for an existing key record), and revocation. The events are emitted for both API and Console-backed workflows and follow the audit schema library introduced in pr-21984 and the event_type registry from pr-21991. Motivation API keys are a high-risk credential surface and are explicitly called out as “sensitive actions” for SOC2/ISO-aligned control evidence and customer investigations. Prior to this PR we had partial/legacy logging with inconsistent field names and (in one path) a debug log that could include a key prefix. This PR standardizes the audit semantics and hardens redaction so key material is never persisted to the audit stream. Event types added (registered in audit registry) - credential.api_key.created - credential.api_key.rotated - credential.api_key.revoked Actor / target semantics - actor.* is the authenticated principal performing the action (user/session, service account, or internal system actor). For Console-initiated actions, the actor is the human user; for API actions, actor is derived from the auth context (user vs service token). - target.* represents the API key record being modified. We intentionally avoid putting any API key secret material in either actor or target fields. Redaction / key material handling - The API key secret (full value) is NEVER included in the audit payload, logs, errors, or traces. - We emit: key_id (server-generated stable identifier) and key_fingerprint (sha256 of the secret, truncated to 12 hex chars) to support correlation without leaking the key. - We also emit key_prefix only in the response payload to the caller at creation time; it is explicitly prohibited in audit events and in application logs. - Added a lint guard that rejects attempted inclusion of fields matching /(api[_-]?key|secret|token)(_value|_raw|_plaintext)?/ in audit event builders for these event types. Payload fields (high-level) Each event includes: schema_version, event_id (ULID), occurred_at, event_type, tenant_id, project_id (if scoped), actor_type/actor_id/actor_email_hash (when available), source_ip, user_agent (capped), request_id, trace_id, target_type=api_key, target_id=key_id, and a small metadata block (flattened) with: api_key_name, api_key_scopes (sorted + allow-listed), expires_at, and for rotate/revoke: previous_state/new_state. Note: Per cardinality budget guidance we do not include arbitrary free-form descriptions; api_key_name is capped at 64 chars and normalized whitespace. Implementation details - Added AuditEmitter hooks in the API key service methods: CreateKey(), RotateKey(), RevokeKey(). - Ensured the emitter is called after DB commit but before response return so audit reflects a successful state transition. (Failures remain covered by standard error logs; we do not emit “attempted” events in v1.) - Added correlation propagation from request context into background tasks used by rotation/revoke for async cleanup, so audit events carry request_id/trace_id consistently. - Normalized legacy fields: where older code used audit_action or resource_uuid, we now use event_type and target_id. Testing - Unit tests: verify each action emits exactly one audit event with required fields; verify fingerprint generation is stable and does not equal any substring of the raw key. - Integration tests: exercise Console workflow paths (via console-backend calling control plane) and direct API calls; assert audit payload validates against v1 JSON schema. - Negative tests: assert audit builder rejects inclusion of api_key_value/secret/token-like fields (regression for near-miss reported in eng-security thread). text: Summary This PR adds canonical v1 audit events for API key lifecycle actions in the control plane: creation, rotation (new key issued for an existing key record), and revocation. The events are emitted for both API and Console-backed workflows and follow the audit schema library introduced in pr-21984 and the event_type registry from pr-21991. Motivation API keys are a high-risk credential surface and are explicitly called out as “sensitive actions” for SOC2/ISO-aligned control evidence and customer investigations. Prior to this PR we had partial/legacy logging with inconsistent field names and (in one path) a debug log that could include a key prefix. This PR standardizes the audit semantics and hardens redaction so key material is never persisted to the audit stream. Event types added (registered in audit registry) - credential.api_key.created - credential.api_key.rotated - credential.api_key.revoked Actor / target semantics - actor.* is the authenticated principal performing the action (user/session, service account, or internal system actor). For Console-initiated actions, the actor is the human user; for API actions, actor is derived from the auth context (user vs service token). - target.* represents the API key record being modified. We intentionally avoid putting any API key secret material in either actor or target fields. Redaction / key material handling - The API key secret (full value) is NEVER included in the audit payload, logs, errors, or traces. - We emit: key_id (server-generated stable identifier) and key_fingerprint (sha256 of the secret, truncated to 12 hex chars) to support correlation without leaking the key. - We also emit key_prefix only in the response payload to the caller at creation time; it is explicitly prohibited in audit events and in application logs. - Added a lint guard that rejects attempted inclusion of fields matching /(api[_-]?key|secret|token)(_value|_raw|_plaintext)?/ in audit event builders for these event types. Payload fields (high-level) Each event includes: schema_version, event_id (ULID), occurred_at, event_type, tenant_id, project_id (if scoped), actor_type/actor_id/actor_email_hash (when available), source_ip, user_agent (capped), request_id, trace_id, target_type=api_key, target_id=key_id, and a small metadata block (flattened) with: api_key_name, api_key_scopes (sorted + allow-listed), expires_at, and for rotate/revoke: previous_state/new_state. Note: Per cardinality budget guidance we do not include arbitrary free-form descriptions; api_key_name is capped at 64 chars and normalized whitespace. Implementation details - Added AuditEmitter hooks in the API key service methods: CreateKey(), RotateKey(), RevokeKey(). - Ensured the emitter is called after DB commit but before response return so audit reflects a successful state transition. (Failures remain covered by standard error logs; we do not emit “attempted” events in v1.) - Added correlation propagation from request context into background tasks used by rotation/revoke for async cleanup, so audit events carry request_id/trace_id consistently. - Normalized legacy fields: where older code used audit_action or resource_uuid, we now use event_type and target_id. Testing - Unit tests: verify each action emits exactly one audit event with required fields; verify fingerprint generation is stable and does not equal any substring of the raw key. - Integration tests: exercise Console workflow paths (via console-backend calling control plane) and direct API calls; assert audit payload validates against v1 JSON schema. - Negative tests: assert audit builder rejects inclusion of api_key_value/secret/token-like fields (regression for near-miss reported in eng-security thread). title: Audit logging: register sensitive-action event types and reject unknown event_type values
preview:
Summary: Introduces a centralized registry for audit log event_type values (sensitive actions taxonomy) and enforces that all emitted audit events use a registered event_type. Enforcement happens in two places: (1) build-time lint/codegen to prevent new event types from being introduced ad-hoc, and (2) runtime validation in the shared audit event emitter to optionally reject (or warn on) unknown types.

Motivation: As we drive to 100% coverage for sensitive actions (ENG-4822), we need consistent semantics across services and deployments (Hosted/Dedicated/Private). In the current state, event_type strings are free-form. This makes SIEM normalization brittle and makes coverage dashboards noisy (slight spelling variants create “new” types). Centralizing the taxonomy also reduces cardinality risk and improves backcompat guarantees.

Design: - Source-of-truth registry lives in repo at audit/event_types/v1/sensitive_actions.json (list of allowed event_type strings + minimal metadata used for linting and docs). - Codegen step produces language-specific constants: Go (internal/audit/types_gen.go) and TS (packages/audit-events/src/eventTypes.gen.ts). - Shared audit library validates event_type during emission: default mode is WARN in dev/test and REJECT in prod (configurable per service via AUDIT_EVENT_TYPE_ENFORCEMENT=warn|reject|off). - CI lint checks: (a) fails PR if a producer emits an event_type not in the registry, (b) fails PR if registry is modified without bumping the generated outputs, and (c) blocks non-enumerated types unless prefixed with "custom." (explicit escape hatch; still logged and surfaced as “non-sensitive/custom”).

Backcompat/rollout: This PR only adds the registry + enforcement hooks; it does not change existing producer strings except for a small handful of known typos in tests. For services that currently emit legacy/unknown values, the runtime validator can be set to WARN temporarily. Follow-up PRs will migrate remaining producers to registry values and flip enforcement to REJECT by default in prod once coverage is validated.

Test plan: - Unit tests: registry parser validation, codegen snapshot checks, and runtime validator behavior (warn vs reject). - Lint test: fixture producer intentionally emitting "rbac.role.updated" fails when registry only allows "rbac.role.update". - Integration: existing audit event schema validation tests continue to pass; this PR does not change schema.

Notes on naming: We’re aligning on <domain>.<resource>.<action> (e.g., rbac.role.update, credential.api_key.rotate, policy.routing.update). This matches the taxonomy draft discussed in #eng-security and keeps Splunk/Sentinel field values stable.

Screenshots/log output: - Example WARN (dev): "audit: unknown event_type=rbac.role.updated; allowed=...; proceeding" - Example REJECT (prod): request fails with 500 only if AUDIT_EMIT_FAILURE_MODE=block; otherwise action proceeds but audit event is dropped with explicit metric increment (see internal/audit/metrics.go).

Follow-ups: - ENG-4823/4824/4825 will update producers to use generated constants rather than raw strings. - ENG-4828 coverage dashboard will use the registry list as expected set (reduces false gaps).

Risk assessment: Low runtime risk when configured to WARN. When set to REJECT+block, misconfigured services could impact admin actions. For that reason this PR keeps default enforcement as WARN unless explicitly set by the service owner.

References: - Confluence draft: “Sensitive actions event taxonomy” and “Audit event schema v1” (workshop notes). - Slack: #eng-security thread on event_type naming + cardinality budget.

title: Add /rollback shortcut command to post rollback UI + runbook links in incident channels
preview:
Title: Add /rollback shortcut command to post rollback UI + runbook links in incident channels

Context
During recent gamedays + the SUP-18421 escalation, the biggest time sink was not executing rollback itself, but getting responders to the right place quickly (Console rollback view + the canonical runbook) while context switching across tabs. This PR adds a tiny “fast path” in incident-bot: a slash command that posts the correct links and a short checklist directly into the incident channel.

What’s included
- New Slack slash command: `/rollback` (alias `/rw-rollback`).
- Posts a message with:
  - Link to Console Rollback UI (Optimization Config history view with the rollback modal entry point).
  - Link to the runbook: “Rollback Optimization Config”.
  - A reminder of the expected decision criteria (p95/p99 regression + 5xx) and the post-rollback verification checklist.
- Optional args:
  - `--env` (default: prod)
  - `--tenant <tenant_slug>`
  - `--model <model_id>`
  - `--region <region>`
  - `--scope <hosted|dedicated|private>` (default: hosted)
  These args don’t mutate anything; they’re used only to build a more specific Console deep link and to display the blast-radius header in the message.
- Safety/guardrails:
  - Only allowed in channels matching `^#incidents(-.*)?$` or `^#oncall(-.*)?$` (configurable via `INCIDENT_BOT_ALLOWED_CHANNELS_REGEX`).
  - If invoked elsewhere, command responds ephemerally with guidance and does not post any internal links.
  - If `--scope private` is provided, we do NOT link to hosted Console rollback UI; we instead post the private operator guide link and the generic runbook section.
  - Requests are logged (structured) with `request_id`, `slack_user_id`, `channel_id`, and parsed args to simplify debugging (no customer payloads).

Why incident-bot vs Console
This is intentionally not part of the rollback product surface area. It’s an operational shortcut for responders to reduce MTTR during active incidents while the Safe Apply + Rollback feature rolls out more broadly (PM-904).

Implementation notes
- Adds a new handler module `commands/rollback.ts` wired into the existing Slack command router.
- Centralizes link construction in `lib/links.ts` to avoid hardcoding Console hostnames across command handlers.
  - Uses `CONSOLE_BASE_URL` and `DOCS_BASE_URL` env vars.
  - Adds `consoleRollbackUrl({ env, tenant, model, region })` helper (returns a deep link with query params when provided).
- Adds a small args parser with strict allowlist flags (reject unknown flags and return help text).
- Includes unit tests for:
  - Channel allow/deny behavior
  - Arg parsing + help
  - Link construction for hosted/dedicated/private scopes

Screenshots (Slack output)
- Example: `/rollback --tenant northbridge --model llama-3.1-70b --region us-east-1`
  Posts:
  - “Rollback links (prod / hosted) — tenant=northbridge model=llama-3.1-70b region=us-east-1”
  - Button: “Open Rollback UI”
  - Button: “Rollback Runbook”
  - Bullet: “After rollback: verify p95 <= baseline + 5xx back to normal; annotate the incident timeline with config_version.”

Testing
- Unit: `pnpm test` (added coverage around new command router path).
- Lint/typecheck: `pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck`.
- Manual:
  - Verified in #incidents-sandbox: message posts correctly and buttons render.
  - Verified in #random: command responds ephemerally and does not post links.

Rollout
- This is behind Slack app permission already granted to incident-bot; no new scopes required.
- Deploy with next incident-bot release (no migration).

Checklist
- [x] Unit tests added for allowlist and arg parsing
- [x] Verified command behavior in non-allowed channels (ephemeral response)
- [x] Links use env-configured base URLs (no hardcoding)
- [x] Reviewed with SRE for incident channel patterns

Commits
1) Add rollback slash command handler + routing
2) Add link helpers + env config
3) Add tests for command allowlist and link output

Changed areas (high level)
- src/commands/rollback.ts
- src/router/commands.ts
- src/lib/links.ts
- src/lib/args.ts
- test/rollback_command.test.ts
- config/env.example

Review conversation
- Sean Gallagher (requested changes): “Can we ensure we don’t leak internal Console URLs into customer shared channels? Some incident channels are bridged.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added channel regex allowlist + explicit deny behavior (ephemeral-only) and made it configurable via env.
- Hannah Rivera: “The copy should remind responders that rollback is a control-plane action and still needs confirmation in Console; avoid implying this command performs rollback.”
  - Aisha Bello: Updated message header + button labels to say ‘Open Rollback UI’ and added ‘This command does not execute rollback’ note.
- Marcus Lin: “Any authz concerns? At minimum, log invocations for auditability.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added structured log event `incident_bot.command.rollback.invoked` with request_id + slack identifiers. No tokens or sensitive params.

CI
- build: pass
- unit-tests: pass
- lint: pass
- typecheck: pass

Merge notes
Squash merged after approvals. No runtime behavior changes outside Slack command surface. Safe to deploy independently of the control-plane rollback feature flag (PM-904), since this only links to UI/docs.

title: Optimize quality gates: add override flow w/ expiration + audit events (admin-only)
preview:
## Summary
Implements the time-bound override workflow for Optimize quality gate failures. This enables an authorized approver to temporarily override a failed gate (or a blocked apply) with an explicit reason, expiration, and an auditable trail.

Key properties:
- Overrides are **time-bound** (required `expires_at`) with max TTL enforcement.
- Only users with an **admin override role** may grant/extend/revoke overrides.
- Overrides are **scoped** (route + model + proposed config diff hash) to avoid blanket bypass.
- All override actions emit **audit events** (request/grant/deny/revoke/expire) and are queryable.
- Optimize apply and rollout promotion paths treat an active override as an explicit exception (still logged).

This PR is part of the “Quality guardrails gating for Optimize” project and lands the last missing piece needed for limited beta (see ENG-3927 / PM-482).

## Motivation
During internal beta we had legitimate cases where a gate fails due to known eval flakiness or an urgent cost/latency incident response. We need a controlled exception mechanism that:
1) does not silently bypass quality gates,
2) expires automatically,
3) has strong RBAC and auditability.

## What’s included
### 1) Data model + state machine
- Adds `quality_gate_overrides` persistence keyed by `(account_id, route_id, model_id, config_diff_hash)`.
- Override state: `REQUESTED` -> `GRANTED` | `DENIED` -> `REVOKED` | `EXPIRED`.
- Enforces: `expires_at` required; `expires_at <= now + MAX_OVERRIDE_TTL` (default 72h; configurable per env).
- Supports `extend` with the same TTL cap; extension requires a new audit event.

### 2) API endpoints (Optimize service)
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:request`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:grant`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:deny`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:revoke`
- `GET  /v1/quality-gates/overrides?account_id=&route_id=&model_id=`

Notes:
- “request” can be made by route editors, but “grant/deny/revoke” requires admin override role.
- All endpoints require an explicit `reason` string and optional `ticket` reference (Linear/Jira).

### 3) Enforcement points
- Apply boundary: if `GateResult == FAILED` and no active override -> apply is blocked (existing behavior from pr-19531). If active override exists -> allow apply but mark as `OVERRIDDEN` and log.
- Rollout coupling (prep): response payload includes `override_status` so rollout controller/Console can show “allowed by override until <ts>”. (Full rollout controller enforcement remains defense-in-depth; this PR ensures Optimize-side enforcement is correct.)

### 4) Audit log events
Emits audit events via the existing audit pipeline:
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.requested`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.granted`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.denied`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.revoked`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.expired`

Event fields include: account_id, actor_id, actor_email, route_id, model_id, config_diff_hash, gate_run_id (if present), expires_at, reason, and linked ticket.

### 5) Admin-only guardrails
- Introduces `RBAC_PERMISSION_OPTIMIZE_GATE_OVERRIDE_APPROVE`.
- Adds server-side checks for:
  - admin role requirement
  - max TTL
  - cannot grant an override for a config diff that fails static validation (we still block unsafe diffs)
  - cannot grant override if gate is `PENDING/RUNNING` (must have a terminal failed result or explicit “apply blocked by gate” context)

### 6) Background expiration
- Adds a lightweight sweeper job (`override_expirer`) that marks overrides as expired and emits `...expired` audit event.
- Expiration is also enforced on read paths (i.e., an override past expiration is treated as inactive even if sweeper is delayed).

## Files/areas changed (high level)
- `services/optimize/api/quality_gates/override_endpoints.ts`
- `services/optimize/core/quality_gates/override_service.ts`
- `services/optimize/core/quality_gates/enforcement.ts`
- `services/optimize/db/migrations/20260122_add_quality_gate_overrides.sql`
- `services/optimize/rbac/permissions.ts`
- `services/optimize/audit/events/quality_gate_override_events.ts`
- `services/optimize/jobs/override_expirer.ts`
- `services/optimize/tests/quality_gates/override_flow.test.ts`

## API response changes
- Gate summary responses now include `override`: `{ status, expires_at, granted_by, granted_at }` (flattened into existing response fields; no breaking change for older clients).
- Apply response includes `applied_by_override=true/false` and `override_id` when relevant.

## Testing
- Unit tests: TTL enforcement, RBAC checks, scoping, and expiration behavior.
- Integration tests: request -> grant -> apply allowed -> audit events emitted; revoke -> apply blocked.
- Added regression test for “expired override treated as inactive even if record exists”.

## Rollout plan
- Behind feature flag `optimize_quality_gate_overrides_enabled` (on by default in staging, off in prod until limited beta).

text:
## Summary
Implements the time-bound override workflow for Optimize quality gate failures. This enables an authorized approver to temporarily override a failed gate (or a blocked apply) with an explicit reason, expiration, and an auditable trail.

Key properties:
- Overrides are **time-bound** (required `expires_at`) with max TTL enforcement.
- Only users with an **admin override role** may grant/extend/revoke overrides.
- Overrides are **scoped** (route + model + proposed config diff hash) to avoid blanket bypass.
- All override actions emit **audit events** (request/grant/deny/revoke/expire) and are queryable.
- Optimize apply and rollout promotion paths treat an active override as an explicit exception (still logged).

This PR is part of the “Quality guardrails gating for Optimize” project and lands the last missing piece needed for limited beta (see ENG-3927 / PM-482).

## Motivation
During internal beta we had legitimate cases where a gate fails due to known eval flakiness or an urgent cost/latency incident response. We need a controlled exception mechanism that:
1) does not silently bypass quality gates,
2) expires automatically,
3) has strong RBAC and auditability.

## What’s included
### 1) Data model + state machine
- Adds `quality_gate_overrides` persistence keyed by `(account_id, route_id, model_id, config_diff_hash)`.
- Override state: `REQUESTED` -> `GRANTED` | `DENIED` -> `REVOKED` | `EXPIRED`.
- Enforces: `expires_at` required; `expires_at <= now + MAX_OVERRIDE_TTL` (default 72h; configurable per env).
- Supports `extend` with the same TTL cap; extension requires a new audit event.

### 2) API endpoints (Optimize service)
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:request`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:grant`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:deny`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:revoke`
- `GET  /v1/quality-gates/overrides?account_id=&route_id=&model_id=`

Notes:
- “request” can be made by route editors, but “grant/deny/revoke” requires admin override role.
- All endpoints require an explicit `reason` string and optional `ticket` reference (Linear/Jira).

### 3) Enforcement points
- Apply boundary: if `GateResult == FAILED` and no active override -> apply is blocked (existing behavior from pr-19531). If active override exists -> allow apply but mark as `OVERRIDDEN` and log.
- Rollout coupling (prep): response payload includes `override_status` so rollout controller/Console can show “allowed by override until <ts>”. (Full rollout controller enforcement remains defense-in-depth; this PR ensures Optimize-side enforcement is correct.)

### 4) Audit log events
Emits audit events via the existing audit pipeline:
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.requested`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.granted`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.denied`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.revoked`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.expired`

Event fields include: account_id, actor_id, actor_email, route_id, model_id, config_diff_hash, gate_run_id (if present), expires_at, reason, and linked ticket.

### 5) Admin-only guardrails
- Introduces `RBAC_PERMISSION_OPTIMIZE_GATE_OVERRIDE_APPROVE`.
- Adds server-side checks for:
  - admin role requirement
  - max TTL
  - cannot grant an override for a config diff that fails static validation (we still block unsafe diffs)
  - cannot grant override if gate is `PENDING/RUNNING` (must have a terminal failed result or explicit “apply blocked by gate” context)

### 6) Background expiration
- Adds a lightweight sweeper job (`override_expirer`) that marks overrides as expired and emits `...expired` audit event.
- Expiration is also enforced on read paths (i.e., an override past expiration is treated as inactive even if sweeper is delayed).

## Files/areas changed (high level)
- `services/optimize/api/quality_gates/override_endpoints.ts`
- `services/optimize/core/quality_gates/override_service.ts`
- `services/optimize/core/quality_gates/enforcement.ts`
- `services/optimize/db/migrations/20260122_add_quality_gate_overrides.sql`
- `services/optimize/rbac/permissions.ts`
- `services/optimize/audit/events/quality_gate_override_events.ts`
- `services/optimize/jobs/override_expirer.ts`
- `services/optimize/tests/quality_gates/override_flow.test.ts`

## API response changes
- Gate summary responses now include `override`: `{ status, expires_at, granted_by, granted_at }` (flattened into existing response fields; no breaking change for older clients).
- Apply response includes `applied_by_override=true/false` and `override_id` when relevant.

## Testing
- Unit tests: TTL enforcement, RBAC checks, scoping, and expiration behavior.
- Integration tests: request -> grant -> apply allowed -> audit events emitted; revoke -> apply blocked.
- Added regression test for “expired override treated as inactive even if record exists”.

## Rollout plan
- Behind feature flag `optimize_quality_gate_overrides_enabled` (on by default in staging, off in prod until limited beta).
- Admin permission assignment tracked via INT-3391.

## Follow-ups
- Console UI wiring for override request/grant prompts is tracked in ENG-3925 / DES-611.
- Export of these audit events to customer-facing audit log UI is tracked separately (Console milestone).

---

**Marcus Lin (Security):** Please ensure granting overrides is admin-only and cannot be used to bypass static safety validation for runtime-unsafe diffs.
**Jordan Lee (Author):** Added RBAC permission gate on grant/deny/revoke endpoints and enforced “static validation must pass” prior to allowing an override to authorize apply.

**Sean Gallagher (SRE):** The expiration sweeper job should be best-effort but enforcement must be correct even if the job is delayed.
**Jordan Lee:** Implemented read-time expiration check; sweeper is only for emitting explicit `expired` audit events + cleanup.

**Michael Nguyen:** We should capture a stable scope key; route/model alone is too broad.
**Jordan Lee:** Scoped overrides to config_diff_hash + route_id + model_id (+ account_id). Added tests verifying override does not apply to a different diff.

**Paula Mendes (PM):** Copy/naming in event types should be consistent with our quality gate state machine naming.
**Jordan Lee:** Updated event names to `optimize.quality_gate.override.*` and added `gate_run_id` when present.

title: Add gate-run artifact links to Console-facing Optimize APIs
preview: Context: Optimize quality gates now produce structured results + artifacts (eval report HTML/JSON, metric breakdowns, prompt-set diff links, rerun provenance). Console needs stable, permissioned references to those artifacts to render the gate results view and the “blocked apply” reason details. This PR extends the Console-facing Optimize API responses to include (a) a compact gate result summary for each recommendation/config-diff and (b) artifact references (URLs or ids) that the UI can link to. What changed: Added `gate_summary` and `artifact_refs` fields to the Optimize recommendation payload and to the gate-run detail endpoint; `gate_summary` includes gate_run_id, state, tier (smoke/full), baseline_id, started_at/completed_at, top-level pass/fail reason codes, and a small set of headline deltas (e.g., task_success_delta, rubric_delta) for UI tables; `artifact_refs` includes report_html_url (short-lived), report_json_url (short-lived), raw_results_blob_id (stable), and prompt_set_run_ids for drilldowns; Implemented signing / access control: by default we return stable ids; for users with route-level read permission we also return presigned URLs (10 min TTL) via the Console API path; Updated OpenAPI schema + SDK types used by Console (internal) to reflect the new fields; Added redaction: internal-only fields (raw s3 keys, evaluator debug traces) are never returned; Included backwards compatibility: existing clients ignore unknown fields; for older Console versions, the response shape is unchanged. Notes: The goal here is UI consumption; this does not change gating decisions or enforcement. Follow-up work: wire UI to prefer `*_blob_id` and fetch signed URLs via `/artifacts/{id}:resolve` once that endpoint lands (tracked in ENG-3925 subtask).

title: Block Optimize config apply when quality gate fails + surface failure reasons
preview:
Context: Optimize recommendations can change serving behavior (batching / caching / quantization profiles). We already persist gate runs and compute pass/fail, but there were still paths where an operator (or automation) could call the apply endpoint even if the gate was failed or still running. This PR enforces quality gating at the final boundary: the ApplyConfig operation.

What this PR does: (1) Adds a mandatory precondition check in OptimizeService ApplyConfig: if the target route/policy requires gating, the request must reference a gate_run_id and that gate run must be in PASSED or OVERRIDDEN state at time of apply. (2) Returns explicit, structured failure reasons (reason_code + human message) and stable identifiers for the UI/automation to link to the gate artifacts. (3) Adds a Console deep-link URL and API field to allow the Console to render “Blocked by quality gate” with one click to inspect the run.

Key behavior changes: - If gate is FAILED => ApplyConfig returns HTTP 409 with code QUALITY_GATE_FAILED, includes gate_run_id, gate_status=FAILED, failing_metrics summary (top 3), and a console_url. - If gate is RUNNING/PENDING => ApplyConfig returns HTTP 409 with code QUALITY_GATE_PENDING and includes a recommended retry_after_seconds. - If gate is missing but required => ApplyConfig returns HTTP 400 with code QUALITY_GATE_REQUIRED (this is the "safe by default" behavior). - If policy does not require gating (legacy/internal routes in staging) => unchanged behavior.

Non-goals: This PR does not implement override approval flows (handled in pr-19537) and does not add per-route policy configurability (handled in pr-19588). It does include forward-compatible fields in the error response to avoid another API churn when those land.

Testing: Added unit tests for gate status transitions and ApplyConfig error mapping; added integration tests to ensure ApplyConfig blocks on FAILED and PENDING gates, and allows PASSED.

Rollout plan: This is guarded behind feature flag optimize.quality_gates.enforce_apply=true (default off). We will enable in staging first, then for internal dogfood routes, then limited beta. Flag will be flipped on alongside Console UI work.

Checklist: - [x] Unit tests - [x] Integration tests - [x] OpenAPI updated - [x] Error codes documented - [x] Structured log added for blocked applies (for audit + debugging)

Refs: ENG-3921 (state machine), ENG-3927 (audit log events), related PRs: pr-19523 (eval-harness wiring), pr-19537 (overrides).

title: Console: add rollback trigger endpoint with server-side confirmation checks
preview:
Adds the Console backend endpoint used by the new “Rollback” button for Optimization Config versions. This PR focuses on server-side confirmation/guardrails so the Console UI can remain thin and we avoid dangerous client-only checks.

Key behavior
- New endpoint: POST /internal/console/optimization-config/rollbacks
  - Triggers a rollback for a given scope (tenant + model + region) to a target config_version_id.
  - Returns a rollback_operation_id and the initial operation state.
- Confirmation model
  - Requires a confirmation payload computed server-side and echoed back by the client (type-to-confirm UX can be driven from this).
  - The server recomputes the confirmation on request and rejects if it doesn’t match (prevents stale modal confirmations).
- Guardrails (server-enforced)
  - Blast-radius summary + limits: rejects requests that exceed allowed scope cardinality for Console-triggered rollback (e.g., wildcard tenant + wildcard model).
  - Cooldown: prevents repeated rollback thrash for the same scope within a configurable window (default 15m) unless caller has override permission.
  - Safety checks: disallow rollback while an apply operation is actively progressing for the same scope (must pause/cancel first).
  - Version checks: cannot rollback to a version that is not in the “promoted/known-good” state unless override is present.
  - Idempotency: supports optional Idempotency-Key header to avoid duplicate rollback operations on retries.
- RBAC / audit
  - Enforces permission optimization_config.rollback at the endpoint.
  - Emits audit events: optimization_config.rollback_requested and optimization_config.rollback_rejected with standardized fields (request_id, actor, scope, from_version, to_version, reason, confirmation_hash).

API details
- Request fields (high level)
  - scope: tenant_id, model_id, region (no wildcards from Console path)
  - target_version_id
  - reason (required; displayed in history/audit)
  - confirmation: { summary_hash, typed_phrase } (typed_phrase is validated against the server-provided phrase)
- Response fields (high level)
  - rollback_operation_id
  - state: queued|running|completed|failed
  - active_version_before
  - target_version
  - effective_cooldown_until (when applicable)

Implementation notes
- Confirmation computation lives in ConsoleRollbackConfirmationService and includes: scope display name(s), current active version, target version, and a deterministic hash of {scope, from, to, cooldown_bucket}.
- Cooldown is stored in control plane as a scope-keyed record with TTL; implemented with a compare-and-set to avoid race conditions under concurrent requests.
- Conflict detection uses the existing rollout/apply operation table: if there is a non-terminal apply operation for the same scope, rollback is rejected with HTTP 409 and a machine-readable error code APPLY_IN_PROGRESS.
- Keeps the endpoint under /internal/console/* for now; public rollback API remains in the control plane service surface (tracked separately) and will call into the same underlying RollbackOrchestrator.

Testing
- Unit tests cover confirmation mismatch, blast-radius rejection, cooldown enforcement, and idempotency.
- Integration tests cover 409 on apply-in-progress and a happy-path rollback request emitting audit events.

Rollout
- Endpoint is feature-flagged (console_optimization_rollback_enabled) and returns 404 when disabled to avoid exposing partial functionality.

Follow-ups
- Hook up Console UI to show effective_cooldown_until and suggested next action (“wait” vs “request override”).
- Add support for Dedicated “shard” scopes once shard-based rollouts land (ENG-4824).

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4828, ENG-4825
- Related audit taxonomy work: ENG-4826
- Addresses internal on-call confusion from INT-3390 by making permission errors explicit and returning required_permission in the error payload.

title: Add Optimization Config change permissions + operator roles (apply/promote/rollback)
preview: Summary: Introduces explicit RBAC permissions for the Optimization Config lifecycle (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback) and adds two opinionated roles used by Console + control plane workflows. This is part of the Safe apply + rollback mechanism project to ensure rollback is both auditable and access-controlled. Motivation: Today, optimization config changes are implicitly covered by broad “platform operator” capabilities in some environments, and not consistently modeled in Private deployments. Enterprise customers and Security review require least-privilege roles with clear separation between “can propose” and “can execute rollback in prod.” Changes included: 1) New permissions: optimization_config.read, optimization_config.create_version, optimization_config.validate, optimization_config.diff, optimization_config.apply, optimization_config.promote, optimization_config.rollback, optimization_config.override_gates (break-glass), optimization_config.manage_rbac (internal only). 2) New roles: optimization_change_operator (intended for on-call + platform operators executing safe apply/rollback within an approved scope; includes read/diff/validate/apply/rollback; does NOT include promote-by-default to reduce accidental broad rollouts), optimization_change_admin (intended for senior operators; includes promote + override_gates; still does not include manage_rbac). 3) Updates to policy docs: clarified mapping from Console actions (“Rollback” button, “Promote to production”, “Override gates”) to permissions and recommended reviewer/approval process. 4) Tests: added rego/policy tests + golden snapshots covering allow/deny cases for Hosted/Dedicated/Private personas, including a regression test for INT-3390 (operator could not see rollback button due to missing permission). Backwards compatibility: No default widening of access; existing roles are unchanged except for documentation noting recommended migration. Control plane/Console will explicitly request these permissions when the feature flag is enabled. Rollout plan: Land policy bundle first; then enable in staging; then gate Console button visibility on optimization_config.rollback + server-side authz checks in control plane. Notes: “override_gates” is explicitly separate and intended for internal break-glass flows only (guarded by additional approval and audited separately).

title: Add break-glass verification override guardrails + required audit annotations
preview: Title: Add break-glass verification override guardrails + required audit annotations. Context: As part of offline bundle signature verification, the installer now supports a policy-gated break-glass flag (see redwood-private-installer pr-24979). This PR adds cluster-side guardrails so that (1) break-glass cannot be enabled silently, (2) use is attributable and exportable via audit logs, and (3) only approved operators can authorize it. Summary of changes: (A) Adds a Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplate + Constraint enforcing required annotations whenever break-glass is enabled via the Redwood Private install metadata ConfigMap (redwood-private/install-metadata) or Helm values rendered into that ConfigMap. Required annotations: redwood.ai/break-glass-ticket (SUP-/INT-/customer ticket id), redwood.ai/break-glass-reason (freeform, min 20 chars), redwood.ai/break-glass-approved-by (corporate email), redwood.ai/break-glass-approved-at (RFC3339 timestamp), redwood.ai/break-glass-expires-at (RFC3339; must be <= 72h from approved-at). Optional but recommended: redwood.ai/break-glass-approver-role (Security|ReleaseEng|SRE). (B) Adds a second constraint that denies enabling break-glass unless the requesting subject is in the allowlisted RBAC group (redwood:break-glass-approvers) or is a ServiceAccount with label redwood.ai/purpose=private-installer and bound to ClusterRole redwood-private-break-glass-approver. (C) Adds a policy exception mechanism for dev/test clusters only: if Namespace has label redwood.ai/environment=dev and redwood.ai/policy-exceptions contains break-glass-guardrails, constraints run in "warn" mode and include a warning message; default remains "deny" for all other environments. (D) Adds a small conftest test suite covering: missing annotations, malformed timestamps, expiry > 72h, approver not allowlisted, and the dev warn-only path. title: Shadow traffic: deterministic request fingerprint + bucketing v1
preview:
Context: Shadow traffic needs consistent comparisons across time windows and across control/shadow targets. The earlier routing mirror MVP used per-request RNG sampling which made deltas noisy and made it hard to correlate control vs shadow outputs for the same logical request. This PR adds a deterministic request fingerprint and a stable bucketing/assignment helper used by the data plane when deciding whether to mirror a request and which shadow target(s) to send it to.

What’s in this PR: 1) Request fingerprinting (fingerprint_v1): Computes a 256-bit fingerprint as HMAC-SHA256(tenant_shadow_secret, canonical_request_bytes). Canonical bytes are derived from a canonical JSON encoding of a strictly limited set of fields: route_id, request_kind (chat/completions/embeddings), model_group (logical), normalized generation params (temperature/top_p/max_tokens/etc), and the request content payload (messages/prompt) after canonicalization (stable key ordering, normalized whitespace for JSON tool args, and explicit UTF-8 encoding). The HMAC key is per-tenant (and per-environment) and is never emitted in telemetry. This ensures: (a) the same logical request maps to the same fingerprint across instances/regions; (b) fingerprints are not reversible without the secret; (c) we do not store raw prompt text to achieve determinism.

2) Bucketing + assignment: Adds helpers for (a) deterministic sampling: sample = (uint64(fingerprint[0:8]) / 2^64) < sample_rate; (b) stable variant selection: variant_index = uint64(fingerprint[8:16]) % num_variants; (c) optional multi-shadow fanout selection using independent lanes: lane_i uses bytes [16+8*i : 24+8*i] to avoid correlated selection when we add more than one shadow target.

3) Collision + privacy notes: Includes a short ADR note in-code and in docs about collision risk and privacy posture. Collision risk: We still treat the fingerprint as an identifier, not a cryptographic guarantee of uniqueness; we store both (fingerprint_prefix_64) and (fingerprint_full_256) internally, but only emit a truncated 96-bit form in telemetry events to reduce cardinality and avoid join keys that are too powerful. For collision math: probability is negligible at our expected sample sizes with 96 bits, but we still design joins to tolerate rare collisions (e.g., shadow pairing includes request_id + timestamp bucket as additional join constraints).

4) Guardrails: The canonicalization step caps included content to 16KB for fingerprinting (after UTF-8 bytes) and includes a fast-path for streaming requests: fingerprint is computed from the initial request envelope only (before any streamed tokens) so we do not block the user path on stream completion.

Why HMAC vs plain hash: A plain SHA256(prompt) can be dictionary-attacked for common prompts; HMAC with a per-tenant secret meaningfully raises the bar and prevents cross-tenant correlation of fingerprints.

Rollout plan: This PR wires the helper behind ShadowTrafficDeterministicBucketing (default off). Routing mirror code will start using this in pr-24833 once merged. No behavior change unless the flag is enabled.

Test plan: Unit tests for canonicalization stability (key ordering, whitespace), determinism across processes, sampling distribution sanity (chi-square vs expected), and variant selection stability. Added a microbenchmark to ensure fingerprinting stays < ~15µs p50 on typical chat payloads (1–4 messages).

title: Control plane: add ShadowTrafficPolicy schema + validation + migration scaffolding
preview: Summary: This PR introduces the control-plane schema for ShadowTrafficPolicy and wiring needed to store/validate it in route/rollout configs. This is the first building block for shadow traffic (mirroring sampled requests to one or more shadow targets) and is intentionally scoped to schema/validation + migration scaffolding only (no data-plane mirroring, no telemetry). Motivation: We need a stable, versioned policy object that can be attached to routes and rollout variants, validated at write time, and safely no-oped until the data plane and telemetry paths are enabled. This keeps the upcoming work (ENG-5402/5403/5404) unblocked while making sure we don’t paint ourselves into a corner on defaults, RBAC, and security-sensitive fields. What’s included: (1) New schema objects: ShadowTrafficPolicy v1 and ShadowTargetRef. (2) Validation: sampling is bounded; max shadow targets enforced; timeouts bounded; mutually exclusive fields validated; required metadata controls validated. (3) Migration scaffolding: adds optional shadow_traffic_policy field to RoutePolicy and RolloutVariantPolicy (behind a feature flag gate) and includes a config migration stub to allow future move/rename without breaking existing configs. (4) Example configs and golden tests for JSON serialization. Non-goals: data plane mirroring behavior (ENG-5402), deterministic fingerprinting/bucketing (ENG-5403), telemetry schema + ingestion (ENG-5404), and retention/redaction implementation (ENG-5411). Design notes / defaults: Default is disabled unless explicitly enabled; fail_open defaults true (shadow path must never impact user response); sampling_mode is “deterministic” but bucketing implementation is not in this PR; shadow request headers default to allowlist-only and we explicitly disallow copying Authorization/Cookie by default; shadow targets are references (model_id / deployment_id / routing_policy_id) to avoid embedding large objects and to keep cardinality sane. Key fields (v1): enabled (bool), sampling_rate (0.0–1.0), sampling_mode (deterministic|bernoulli), max_shadow_targets (enforced globally), shadow_targets[] (target_ref + weight + per-target timeout_ms), request_copy (allowlist headers + metadata keys), fail_open (bool), shadow_deadline_ms (overall), tags (limited set; validated against allowlisted keys), created_by/updated_by (populated by control-plane). Tests: adds schema validation unit tests (happy path + boundary/invalid cases), JSON round-trip tests, and a migration test that ensures older configs without shadow_traffic_policy still parse with identical semantics. Rollout plan: This lands gated; no runtime behavior change until the routing layer consumes the policy (ENG-5402). After merge, we’ll start dogfooding in internal tenants with feature flag enabled in the control plane only.
