title: Emit structured audit log events for Optimization Config lifecycle (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback) + schema updates
preview: Context: Optimization config changes (batching/caching/quantization profiles) are production-impacting and must be traceable and reviewable. We’ve had gaps reported by enterprise customers (SUP-18544) and internal operators (INT-3390) where rollback actions were not consistently visible in audit exports, and some lifecycle steps were only logged in app logs. This PR standardizes and emits structured audit events for the full Optimization Config lifecycle and updates the audit schema registry accordingly. What’s included: (1) New/standardized event names under the "optimization_config" namespace: optimization_config.version_created, optimization_config.version_validated, optimization_config.apply_requested, optimization_config.rollout_started, optimization_config.rollout_paused, optimization_config.rollout_resumed, optimization_config.rollout_step_completed, optimization_config.rollout_failed, optimization_config.version_promoted, optimization_config.rollback_requested, optimization_config.rollback_completed, optimization_config.rollback_failed. (2) Consistent required fields across events: event_id, occurred_at, actor_type (user|service), actor_id, actor_email (when actor_type=user), actor_roles (best-effort snapshot), source (console|api|controller), request_id, trace_id (if present), tenant_id, scope (tenant/model/region; serialized as strings), optimization_config_id, optimization_config_version_id, from_version_id/to_version_id (when applicable), rollout_id (for staged apply), canary_percent (when applicable), reason (free-form operator reason; required for rollback_requested), gate_summary (serialized compact string: "latency_p95:pass;5xx:fail" etc.), eval_run_id (only for quantization-related applies where eval gating was invoked), and outcome (success|failure) with error_code/error_message when failure. (3) Explicit PII/secret handling: the audit payload does NOT include raw config bodies, prompt data, or customer inputs; the only config-related identifiers are config_id/version_id and a small "diff_fingerprint" (sha256 of normalized diff) for correlation. (4) Event emission points: service-layer emission in Optimization Config Service for create/validate/apply/promote/rollback API endpoints; rollout controller emits step/progression and gate evaluation results; rollback controller emits completion/failure. (5) Schema updates: updated audit schema registry entries for all new events, plus tightened validation rules for required fields and enums; added forward-compatible optional fields for private deployments (cluster_id, deployment_id) without making them required. (6) Backfill/compat: kept prior legacy event names as aliases for 30 days (emitting both) behind feature flag AUDIT_OPT_CONFIG_DUAL_EMIT; Console and downstream export pipelines continue to function. Example payload (redacted): {"event_name":"optimization_config.rollback_requested","actor_type":"user","actor_id":"u_7f...","actor_email":"oncall@redwoodinference.com","source":"console","tenant_id":"t_123","scope":"model=llama-3.1-70b;region=us-east-1","optimization_config_id":"ocfg_91...","optimization_config_version_id":"v_42","from_version_id":"v_43","to_version_id":"v_42","reason":"p95 regression during canary","request_id":"req_...","trace_id":"tr_..."}. Testing/verification: unit tests for event construction and schema validation; integration tests asserting audit emission on each endpoint; controller tests verifying pause/resume and failure paths emit the right events; manual verification in staging that events appear in SIEM-export sink with correct field names. Rollout plan: ship behind AUDIT_OPT_CONFIG_EVENTS_V1 (default off); enable for internal beta tenants first; after 1 week with no schema errors in export, disable dual-emit and remove aliases in follow-up PR. Checklist: [x] event taxonomy reviewed with Security (Marcus) [x] fields aligned with RBAC expectations (Jordan) [x] no raw config bodies logged [x] added docs snippet to internal audit-event catalog [x] added dashboard annotation hooks is tracked separately (observability-pack PR).

title: Add rollback endpoint for Optimization Config versions (authz + audit events)
preview: Motivation: We need a safe, auditable way to revert optimization changes (batching/caching/quant profiles) without requiring a manual DB edit or redeploy. This PR adds the initial rollback API endpoint to the Optimization Config Service, including RBAC authorization, audit-log emission, and integration tests that codify the expected semantics for callers (Console + internal tooling). API: Adds POST /v1/optimization-configs/rollbacks to request a rollback for a given scope (tenant/model/region) to a target config_version_id (or "previous_promoted" shortcut). The endpoint creates a rollback operation record, performs server-side validation (scope match, version exists/visible, cooldown, and active rollout compatibility), and returns an operation_id that the rollout controller/runtime applier will observe. Authz: Enforces new permission "optimization_config.rollback" and validates caller can act on the requested scope; maps to Console "Optimization change operator" role. Audit: Emits structured audit events for rollback_requested and rollback_failed (validation/authz). Includes request_id, actor, scope, from_version, to_version, operation_id, and reason. Tests: Adds integration tests for (1) happy path rollback with audit event, (2) permission denied returns 403 and emits authz audit, (3) idempotent retry using Idempotency-Key returns same operation_id, (4) invalid target version returns 400 with actionable message and rollback_failed audit. Notes: This PR does not implement the runtime rollback execution path; it only creates the operation + emits events. Runtime/application handling is in subsequent PRs (apply transaction + canary controller).

title: Emit audit event on successful offline bundle verification (Redwood Private)
preview:
Summary: Add a first-class audit event for offline bundle verification successes and wire an emission path that Private/on-prem deployments can use for verification reporting. This complements existing failure events emitted by the installer/preflight tooling and enables customers to prove (locally, and optionally exported) that an installation/upgrade was performed from a bundle that verified cleanly.

Context: As part of the on-prem offline artifact signing work, Support/Solutions and customer security teams requested a positive attestation signal in the audit trail. Today we record failures (hash mismatch / signature invalid / unknown key), but there is no canonical success event that includes the bundle identity + verification metadata. This PR adds the event definition to the redwood audit event catalog and provides a helper to emit it from Private installer pathways that call into redwood audit logging.

What’s in this PR: 1) New audit event definition: private.bundle_verification.succeeded. 2) Shared payload schema + validation with conservative defaults (no raw filesystem paths, no customer data). 3) Emission helper: emitBundleVerificationSucceeded(...) which takes the signed manifest metadata (bundle_build_id, private_release_version, manifest_digest, signer_key_id) and a small set of environment fields (cluster_id if available, install_mode, airgapped=true/false). 4) Unit tests for schema/required fields and redaction rules. 5) Docs/comments in-code pointing to the verification audit events spec and exporter mappings.

Event details: Event name: private.bundle_verification.succeeded. Severity: INFO. Stability: GA (but field additions are backward compatible). Required fields: bundle_build_id, private_release_version, manifest_sha256, signer_key_id, verification_mode (preflight|install|upgrade), result=success. Optional fields: trust_store_version, installer_version (if provided by caller), cluster_id (if available), downgrade_prevented=false/true (future; not emitted here), timestamp (standard). Explicitly excluded: absolute bundle paths, per-file hash lists, raw certificates, customer org identifiers.

Why this lives in `redwood` (not installer repo): The audit event catalog + exporter integration points are owned by the core product, and downstream components (installer, audit-log-exporter, support bundle tooling) rely on consistent event naming and schema guarantees.

Rollout/compat: Backward compatible. The new event is additive; consumers that don’t recognize it will ignore it. Private deployments that want to export this to SIEM should update audit-log-exporter (tracked separately) to map/forward the new event name.

Testing: Added unit tests for schema validation and redaction; updated audit event snapshot tests; verified locally with a simulated preflight call path.

Follow-ups: - Wire this emission from redwood-private-installer once the preflight verification path is finalized (tracked in installer PRs). - Add exporter mapping in audit-log-exporter repo (already in progress).

title: Add least-privilege RBAC for release signing service operator
preview:
## Summary
Introduce a dedicated, least-privilege RBAC role for "release signing operators" who need to initiate and monitor offline bundle signing runs for Redwood Private releases.

This PR removes the current ad-hoc guidance of granting broad permissions in the release-tools cluster and replaces it with an explicit, reviewable policy bundle:
- New `ClusterRole` + namespace-scoped bindings for `release-signing-operator`
- Separation-of-duties: operator can create/approve signing requests and observe jobs, but cannot read signing key material (Secrets) or mutate trust roots
- Policy tests to prevent accidental wildcard RBAC in this area

Context: offline bundle signing pipeline workstream (ENG-4822) + trust store/key rotation migration (ENG-4825). This is intended to be deployed alongside the signing service rollout in the release-tools cluster.

## Motivation / background
As part of on-prem offline artifact signing, we’re introducing a signing service that produces detached signatures for offline bundles. The human/operator flow is: start a signing run, monitor status, and fetch resulting artifacts from the artifact store. Historically this has been done with elevated cluster access (sometimes effectively admin in `release-tools`), which is incompatible with our supply-chain threat model and the evidence expectations we’ve seen from air-gapped/regulated customers.

Threat model alignment:
- Reduce blast radius of compromised operator credentials
- Ensure operators cannot access private key material or modify signing configuration/trust roots
- Improve auditability: explicit role name and bindings show up cleanly in access reviews

## What changed
### New RBAC objects
- `clusters/production/release-tools/rbac/release-signing-operator.clusterrole.yaml`
- `clusters/production/release-tools/rbac/release-signing-operator.rolebinding.yaml`
- `clusters/production/release-tools/rbac/release-signing-operator.serviceaccount.yaml`

Permissions granted (high level):
- Create/update/get/list/watch `signingrequests.signing.redwood.ai` (CRD) in `release-signing` namespace
- Get/list/watch Jobs/Pods in `release-signing` namespace for run status
- Get/list Events in `release-signing` namespace
- Read ConfigMaps used for non-secret job parameters

Explicitly NOT granted:
- No `secrets` access (get/list/watch)
- No write access to ConfigMaps that define trust roots (`signing-trust-*`)
- No cluster-scoped writes beyond the specific CRD

### Removed/cleaned up
- Dropped legacy `release-operator` binding that included broad verbs over core resources in the namespace.

### Policy/CI guardrails
- Add `conftest` rules to fail on:
  - Wildcard resources/verbs in signing operator roles
  - Any Secrets access from roles matching `*signing*operator*`

## Example manifests (illustrative)
`release-signing-operator.clusterrole.yaml` (excerpt)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: release-signing-operator
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["signing.redwood.ai"]
    resources: ["signingrequests"]
    verbs: ["create", "get", "list", "watch", "update", "patch"]
  - apiGroups: ["batch"]
    resources: ["jobs"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "events", "configmaps"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

`release-signing-operator.rolebinding.yaml` (excerpt)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: release-signing-operator
  namespace: release-signing
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: release-signing-operators
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: release-signing-operator
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

## Operational notes
- This PR assumes a distinct `release-signing` namespace (already present in release-tools).
- Group `release-signing-operators` maps to the subset of Release Engineering on-call who initiates signing runs.
- Security admins retain separate, tighter roles for key ceremony operations; those bindings are out of scope here.

## Rollout plan
1) Merge policy bundle
2) Apply to staging release-tools and validate:
   - Operator can submit SigningRequest
   - Operator cannot read any Secret in `release-signing`
3) Apply to prod release-tools
4) Update internal access request template to point to `release-signing-operator` (tracked in INT-11021)

## Testing
- `make lint` (kustomize + yaml lint)
- `make test` (conftest policy checks)
- `kustomize build clusters/production/release-tools | kubeconform` (schema validation)

## Release notes
- Security: add least-privilege RBAC role `release-signing-operator` for offline bundle signing operations in release-tools

---
### Commits
1. rbac: add release-signing-operator ClusterRole + bindings
2. rbac: remove legacy release-operator binding from release-signing namespace
3. policy: add conftest checks preventing wildcard + secrets access for signing operator roles
4. docs: add README note for requesting signing operator access

---
### Review conversation (abridged)
Marcus Lin (Security Eng): "Please ensure the operator role has *no* access to Secrets, including indirect access via `*` in core API groups. Also, can we avoid `update` on ConfigMaps to prevent trust-root drift?"
Devon Price (author): "Agreed. Removed any Secrets verbs (none added, but added conftest to block regressions). Kept ConfigMaps read-only and confirmed the trust-root configmaps are owned by a separate admin role."

Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): "We need to be able to watch Pods and Jobs to drive the signing run status UI + troubleshooting. `pods/log` would be useful, but I’m okay deferring if it creates risk."
Devon Price (author): "Added get/list/watch for Pods/Jobs/Events. Not adding `pods/log` in this PR; we can add a separate, scoped role if needed with explicit justification + audit expectations."

Sahana Iyer (Build/CI): "Conftest check failed on first pass because the rule matched the old role too. Looks good now after removing legacy binding."
Devon Price (author): "Fixed by deleting the legacy rolebinding and scoping the conftest selector to `release-signing-operator` resources."

Hanae Suzuki (KMS/Secrets): "Thanks for keeping Secrets out of operator path. Please also ensure there’s no access to `serviceaccounts/token` subresource." 
Devon Price (author): "Confirmed: no SA token subresource permissions. Added a small comment in the manifest to call this out."

Approvals:
- Marcus Lin: Approved
- Connor O'Brien: Approved
- Sahana Iyer: Approved

---
### CI status
- build/kustomize: PASS
- lint/yamllint: PASS
- policy/conftest: PASS (rerun after rule fix)
- validate/kubeconform: PASS

Merged via squash into `main`.

title: Expose Optimization Config Versions API (list/get/diff) for Console + tooling
preview: This PR adds the read-only API surface for Optimization Config versioning: list versions (with filters + pagination), fetch version details, and retrieve diffs between versions. These endpoints are required for the Console history/timeline and the upcoming safe-apply workflow, and they are also used by internal automation (release tooling / incident response) to quickly answer “what config is active for tenant/model/region?” and “what changed between v12 and v13?”. Scope: hosted + dedicated control-plane APIs (private installer/helm wiring will land separately). Non-goals: create/apply/promote/rollback are not included here; this PR is strictly read-only + diff retrieval and enforces RBAC. API additions: (1) Public (customer/admin) endpoints under /v1/optimize/configs: GET /v1/optimize/configs/{config_id}/versions?scope=...&limit=...&cursor=... returns immutable version metadata sorted by created_at desc; GET /v1/optimize/configs/{config_id}/versions/{version_id} returns the full version record including validated schema, semantic warnings (if any), and resolved scope; GET /v1/optimize/configs/{config_id}/versions/diff?from={version_id}&to={version_id} returns a structured diff with “risky fields” annotations for Console highlighting. (2) Internal endpoints under /internal/optimize/configs for ops tooling include an additional query param include_deleted=true and return internal-only fields (storage IDs, writer actor IDs) gated by internal auth. Behavior notes: - Pagination uses an opaque cursor (base64-encoded keyset token). - The list endpoint supports filters: tenant_id, model_id, region, status (draft/validated/active/rolled_back), and time range (created_after/before). - Diff endpoint uses the same canonicalization logic as the validator to avoid noisy diffs (e.g., defaulted fields). - RBAC: requires optimize.config_versions.read for list/get/diff. Internal endpoints require redwood.internal plus optimize.config_versions.read. - Audit: this PR does NOT emit audit events (read-only), but it does attach request_id and actor to structured logs for traceability. Example (public) list response (abridged): {"items":[{"version_id":"ocv_01J...","created_at":"2025-12-01T19:22:11Z","created_by":"user_...","status":"validated","scope":{"tenant_id":"t_123","model_id":"m_llama3_70b","region":"us-east"},"summary":{"batching":{"max_batch_tokens":8192},"cache":{"prefix_cache":true},"quantization":{"profile":"q8_2025_11_30"}}}],"next_cursor":"eyJrZXkiOiJvY3ZfMDFKLi4uIn0="}. Console requirements covered: stable ordering, ability to fetch full config payload for a version, and a deterministic diff output for the diff view. Tests included: handler unit tests (authz + filtering + pagination), API contract tests (OpenAPI snapshot), and diff canonicalization tests. Follow-ups: create/apply endpoints (ENG-4824/4825) will reuse the same response types and pagination helpers; we should consider adding ETag support once Console caching is wired.

title: Demo tenant: scope API keys to least privilege + rotation helpers + audit log coverage
preview: Context: SEs and AEs have been sharing a single broadly-permissioned demo API key for convenience. This has caused a few recurring problems: (1) unclear blast radius when a key leaks in a screen share/recording, (2) brittle access when RBAC mappings change (INT-993), (3) hard-to-debug 429s / quota overrides (SUP-4127) because traffic sources are indistinguishable, and (4) audit log gaps for key lifecycle events. This PR tightens demo-tenant API key scopes to least privilege, adds rotation helpers for the demo reset flow, and ensures we emit consistent audit-log events for key create/rotate/revoke. Summary of changes (single-tenant + parallel demos friendly): 1) New scoped key model for demo workflows: - Introduce explicit permission bundles for demo use cases: `demo.chat.invoke`, `demo.embeddings.invoke`, `demo.routes.read`, `demo.console.read`, `demo.eval.run` (internal), `demo.traffic.seed` (job-only). - Scope keys by (org_id, project_id) and optionally (route_id); default is project scope + specific route allowlist for the standard demo routes. - Disallow admin/control-plane mutations from demo keys by default (no model pin edits, no org role changes, no billing settings). - Add optional `--ttl` for short-lived keys (defaults to 24h for SE-run keys, 2h for externally shared “live demo” keys). 2) Rotation + safe sharing helpers: - Add `rw demo keys create` and `rw demo keys rotate` helpers used by the reset runbook. - Rotation is implemented as: create new key -> update demo app secret refs -> revoke old key (with a 10 minute overlap option). - Adds a `rw demo keys verify-audit` command that checks for expected audit log events over the last N minutes and prints pass/fail with reasons. - Adds guardrails to prevent generating demo keys outside the demo org (requires `DEMO_ORG_ID` match and `--i-know-what-im-doing` escape hatch for on-call). 3) Audit logging: - Emit audit events for `api_key.created`, `api_key.rotated`, `api_key.revoked` with consistent fields: actor_id, actor_type (user/service), org_id, project_id, key_id (opaque), scope_summary, ttl_seconds, reason, ip/user_agent (when applicable). - Ensure rotations emit both `created` (new key) and `revoked` (old key) with `rotation_id` correlation for easier demo incident review. - Add a small backfill shim in the audit pipeline for demo keys created via legacy endpoints (so we don’t regress reporting during rollout). 4) Migration/compatibility: - Existing demo keys continue to work but are marked as `legacy_unscoped=true` and will be refused by `rw demo reset` after 2025-12-15 (soft enforcement: warning now, hard error later). - The demo app (redwood-examples) is unchanged in this PR; it only relies on the hosted inference endpoints. However, the default demo-tenant bootstrap now provisions route-specific keys so the app doesn’t require broad project permissions. Why this approach: - We need demo keys to be shareable (screen share) but low-risk. Route allowlists plus short TTL makes accidental exposure less consequential. - Separating “seed traffic” permissions keeps the synthetic traffic generator from inheriting unnecessary inference permissions. - Audit log correlation makes it easy to answer security-review questions live (“show me key creation, who did it, and when”). Testing: - Unit tests for scope evaluation (project + route allowlist + operation) and TTL enforcement. - Integration test: create scoped key -> call chat completion on allowed route -> expect 200; call embeddings on disallowed route -> expect 403 with `scope_denied`. - Integration test: rotate key -> old key revoked -> verify audit events exist with same rotation_id. - Manual: Markus ran through the SE demo runbook on staging demo tenant; verified the console read-only key can view dashboards but cannot edit routes/models. Rollout plan: - Merge behind feature flag `demo_scoped_keys_default=true` for demo org only. - Enable in demo prod tenant after PR-28531 healthcheck rollout so SEs have a single pass/fail signal. - Communicate to GTM: new default keys are time-bound; reset tooling will automatically refresh them. Security considerations: - No customer data impacted (demo-only). - Avoid logging raw API key material anywhere (only key_id and scope summary). - Audit event payload intentionally excludes key prefixes. Follow-ups: - Add UI affordance in Console to show “scope summary” for a key (read-only) for SE demos (tracked in ENG-4855 subtask). - Update internal demo docs/runbook with the new commands (Elliot to pick up once merged).

default is project scope + specific route allowlist for the standard demo routes. - Disallow admin/control-plane mutations from demo keys by default (no model pin edits, no org role changes, no billing settings). - Add optional `--ttl` for short-lived keys (defaults to 24h for SE-run keys, 2h for externally shared “live demo” keys). 2) Rotation + safe sharing helpers: - Add `rw demo keys create` and `rw demo keys rotate` helpers used by the reset runbook. - Rotation is implemented as: create new key -> update demo app secret refs -> revoke old key (with a 10 minute overlap option). - Adds a `rw demo keys verify-audit` command that checks for expected audit log events over the last N minutes and prints pass/fail with reasons. - Adds guardrails to prevent generating demo keys outside the demo org (requires `DEMO_ORG_ID` match and `--i-know-what-im-doing` escape hatch for on-call). 3) Audit logging: - Emit audit events for `api_key.created`, `api_key.rotated`, `api_key.revoked` with consistent fields: actor_id, actor_type (user/service), org_id, project_id, key_id (opaque), scope_summary, ttl_seconds, reason, ip/user_agent (when applicable). - Ensure rotations emit both `created` (new key) and `revoked` (old key) with `rotation_id` correlation for easier demo incident review. - Add a small backfill shim in the audit pipeline for demo keys created via legacy endpoints (so we don’t regress reporting during rollout). 4) Migration/compatibility: - Existing demo keys continue to work but are marked as `legacy_unscoped=true` and will be refused by `rw demo reset` after 2025-12-15 (soft enforcement: warning now, hard error later). - The demo app (redwood-examples) is unchanged in this PR; it only relies on the hosted inference endpoints. However, the default demo-tenant bootstrap now provisions route-specific keys so the app doesn’t require broad project permissions. Why this approach: - We need demo keys to be shareable (screen share) but low-risk. Route allowlists plus short TTL makes accidental exposure less consequential. - Separating “seed traffic” permissions keeps the synthetic traffic generator from inheriting unnecessary inference permissions. - Audit log correlation makes it easy to answer security-review questions live (“show me key creation, who did it, and when”). Testing: - Unit tests for scope evaluation (project + route allowlist + operation) and TTL enforcement. - Integration test: create scoped key -> call chat completion on allowed route -> expect 200; call embeddings on disallowed route -> expect 403 with `scope_denied`. - Integration test: rotate key -> old key revoked -> verify audit events exist with same rotation_id. - Manual: Markus ran through the SE demo runbook on staging demo tenant; verified the console read-only key can view dashboards but cannot edit routes/models. Rollout plan: - Merge behind feature flag `demo_scoped_keys_default=true` for demo org only. - Enable in demo prod tenant after PR-28531 healthcheck rollout so SEs have a single pass/fail signal. - Communicate to GTM: new default keys are time-bound; reset tooling will automatically refresh them. Security considerations: - No customer data impacted (demo-only). - Avoid logging raw API key material anywhere (only key_id and scope summary). - Audit event payload intentionally excludes key prefixes. Follow-ups: - Add UI affordance in Console to show “scope summary” for a key (read-only) for SE demos (tracked in ENG-4855 subtask). - Update internal demo docs/runbook with the new commands (Elliot to pick up once merged).

title: Add KMS throttle budget + request backpressure to prevent KMS saturation under spikes
preview:
Context: We’ve seen intermittent elevated 5xx correlated with upstream KMS throttling (SUP-19377) and a near-miss retry amplification event that required a hotfix (pr-18563). While the wrapper now has sane retry/jitter defaults and circuit breaking, we still had a failure mode where a large burst of decrypt/envelope ops (e.g. cache cold start + traffic spike) could saturate KMS and extend the incident blast radius.

This PR introduces an explicit "KMS throttle budget" and integrates KMS calls with the existing request admission/backpressure path so we shed/queue work earlier rather than stampeding KMS.

What’s included (high level): (1) Per-process token-bucket budget for KMS operations: A lightweight budget that is decremented on KMS requests and refilled at a configured rate. When depleted, KMS calls are delayed (bounded) or rejected with a typed error depending on caller policy. This keeps concurrency and request rate within a predictable envelope even if upstream is degraded. (2) Adaptive refill + penalty on throttling: When KMS returns throttling (e.g., AWS ThrottlingException / 429 equivalents), we apply an immediate penalty to the budget refill rate for a short window. This quickly reduces pressure and avoids repeated throttles. (3) Backpressure integration: The KMS client wrapper now plugs into `admission.Controller` so callers can either: (a) block up to `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` and then fail fast, or (b) fail immediately (for latency-sensitive paths) with a clear error category (`KmsBudgetExhausted`). (4) Metrics + tracing: Exposes budget state and decisions, and tags spans with the backpressure outcome.

Config knobs (all have safe defaults; prod guardrails still enforced by pr-18502): - `kms.budget.refill_rps` (default: 200 ops/sec) - `kms.budget.burst` (default: 400 ops) - `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` (default: 50ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_window_ms` (default: 30000ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_factor` (default: 0.5)

Behavioral notes: - This budget is per-process (not global) by design. It limits local amplification and works well with our horizontal scaling/admission control. If we need global limits later, we can layer it via control plane config + per-pool budgets. - We do NOT silently drop decrypts. If we can’t meet the wait bound, we return a typed error and let the caller’s policy decide whether to fail the request, fall back, or rely on cached material (where allowed). - When combined with the circuit breaker, the system now transitions more cleanly: budget starts slowing requests first; if KMS remains degraded, the breaker opens and callers follow degrade-mode policy.

Implementation details: - `kmsbudget.Bucket`: token-bucket with penalty hooks; uses monotonic clock; jittered sleeps to avoid herd effects at refill boundaries. - `kmsclient.Client`: wraps `Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey` calls with `Acquire(ctx)`; all outcomes emit structured logs and metrics. - `admission.Controller`: added a dependency-specific limiter hook (KMS only for now) so we can reuse the backpressure path without leaking KMS specifics into product code.

Metrics added: - `redwood_kms_budget_tokens{env,region,service}` (gauge) - `redwood_kms_budget_acquire_total{outcome="immediate|waited|timeout|rejected"}` (counter) - `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms` (histogram) - `redwood_kms_throttle_penalty_active` (gauge) - Existing `redwood_kms_errors_total{category=...}` is now incremented for `BudgetExhausted` and `BackpressureTimeout` (maps to the canonical taxonomy).

Example log fields (new/standardized): `kms_op`, `kms_budget_outcome`, `kms_budget_wait_ms`, `kms_budget_tokens_after`, `kms_error_category`, `trace_id`.

Testing: - Unit tests for token bucket correctness and penalty behavior (including time skews). - Integration harness updated to simulate 429/throttling and verify that request rate to the KMS stub decreases within 2s and that tail latency stays bounded (ties into ENG-4825). - Added a regression test ensuring we don’t exceed retry+budget wait combined latency cap.

Rollout plan: - Behind `kms.backpressure.enabled` (default ON in staging, OFF in prod until we flip per environment). - Canary in us-east Dedicated + Hosted data-plane: monitor `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms p95`, KMS 429 rate, and request 5xx. - Flip prod flag gradually per region; rollback is a single flag change.

Checklist: - [x] SRE reviewed paging/threshold implications (no new pages by default; dashboards use derived panels) - [x] Security reviewed failure semantics (no weakening of envelope decrypt correctness) - [x] Added metrics + trace tags per observability conventions - [x] Added tests for throttle + backpressure scenarios

Follow-ups: - Consider exposing recommended budget defaults in the Private operator guide once we validate prod behavior across 2+ regions. - Evaluate whether control plane should push pool-specific budgets for Dedicated customers with known KMS quotas.

Related: ENG-4821, ENG-4825, ENG-4828; incident context SUP-19377; complements pr-18563 (retry storm hotfix), pr-18449 (circuit breaker), pr-18502 (config guardrails).

title: Add config guardrails for KMS timeout/retry budgets (prod-safe defaults + explicit override)
preview:
Context: As part of the KMS hardening work (ADR-007) and following the recent near-miss retry amplification behavior, we need a hard stop on “retry storm by config.” We currently allow operators to dial up timeouts/retries in config, which in prod can turn a transient KMS degradation into cascading request pileups.

What this PR does: Introduces validation + guardrails for KMS client config so overly aggressive timeout/retry settings cannot be enabled in production without an explicit, time-bound override. This is enforced in two places: (1) control-plane config validation when publishing/rolling out config; (2) service startup validation as a backstop for manually edited configs/private installs.

Guardrails (prod): - kms.timeout_ms must be within [200, 2000]. - kms.max_retries must be within [0, 4]. - kms.max_retry_elapsed_ms must be within [0, 5000]. - computed “worst-case budget” must be <= 7000ms (timeout + backoff + retries), otherwise rejected. - retry backoff must include jitter (enforced via kms.backoff_jitter=true; default true).

Non-prod (dev/staging): Looser caps to support testing, but still rejects obviously broken values (negative, overflow, >60s budgets).

Explicit override: Adds kms.allow_unsafe_retry_settings (default false). In production, enabling this requires: - kms.unsafe_override_reason (must include a ticket reference, e.g., INC- / ENG- / SUP-). - kms.unsafe_override_expires_at (RFC3339 date; must be <= 14 days). If the override is expired or missing fields, config publish fails and service startup fails.

Why both control-plane + startup checks: We’ve seen config drift in Private environments and we want defense-in-depth. Control-plane validation provides the best operator experience; startup validation ensures safety if config reaches a workload via a non-standard path.

Implementation notes: - Adds KmsClientConfigValidator with a canonical “effective budget” calculator used by both control-plane and runtime. - Error messages are operator-actionable and include the exact fields/values that violated limits, plus a short remediation hint. - Validation emits structured logs and a counter metric (kms.config_rejected_total{reason=...}) to make “why did this rollout fail?” visible.

Testing: - Unit tests for boundary conditions and “budget math” (including jitter flag). - A small integration test that ensures prod mode rejects unsafe config unless override fields are present + unexpired.

Related work: - PR #18421 (KMS client abstraction) - PR #18449 (circuit breaker + degrade policy) - Hotfix #18563 (reduce retry amplification) - This PR focuses only on config guardrails; it does not change default retry behavior beyond tightening prod caps + making unsafe configurations opt-in with an expiry.

Rollout: - Land behind default-safe behavior (no config changes required). - If any existing prod environments are currently outside the new caps, they will fail validation at next config publish; we’ve checked current prod configs in us-east/us-west/eu-central and they are within bounds. - SRE requested adding a short note to the on-call KMS runbook about the override path; that update is included in this PR.

title: Introduce credential-harmonizer and backpressure-shim across Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs
preview:
Context and motivation: We see two operational issues that cause instability at scale: (1) SDKs often trigger near-simultaneous credential refreshes when TTL windows align, producing spikes and transient 5xx cascades; (2) long-lived streaming consumers lack a compact way to communicate backpressure to the runtime, which leads to unbounded client queues and memory pressure in bursty workloads. This PR implements a light-weight, opt-in Credential Harmonizer and a Backpressure Shim across Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs to harmonize refresh signals and expose consumer pressure hints.

What this PR does: - Introduces a Credential Harmonizer protocol and local in-process coordinator in each SDK that:  * deduplicates concurrent refresh attempts for the same logical session  * jitter-aligns refresh windows across instances to avoid refresh storms  * surfaces a small, debuggable trace/span when harmonization takes place  * provides a pluggable hook so platform teams can replace the local coordinator with a remote leader-election mechanism if desired. - Adds a Backpressure Shim: a tiny adapter that sits between the SDK stream iterator and the application consumer and:  * emits backpressure hints (slow, steady, clogged) via existing stream telemetry hooks  * exposes a simple API to pause/resume internal receive loops and to temporarily lower prefetch windows  * integrates with existing retry/heartbeat policies to avoid spurious reconnects when consumer-side slowness is detected. - SDK ergonomics:  * Python: new SessionFactory.credential_harmonizer param and StreamHandle.set_backpressure_hint API; bump py SDK minor to 1.28.0.  * TypeScript: exported CredentialHarmonizer, BackpressureShim utilities; minor TS types clarified; bump ts SDK to 1.19.0.  * Go: added cred/harmonizer package, StreamAdapter.PauseHint; bump go SDK to v0.9.4. - Tests and docs: unit tests for harmonizer dedupe and TTL jitter, an integration test that simulates 1k clients with skewed TTLs, updated SDK CHANGELOGs and a short HOWTO in docs/embedding/backpressure.md.

Checklist: - [x] Implementation for Python, TypeScript, Go - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] Documentation and changelog entries - [x] Backwards-compatible defaults (feature is opt-in) - [x] Benchmarks demonstrating reduced refresh traffic spikes

Commits (high-level): - a1b2c3d: initial harmonizer prototypes and cross-sdk interface sketches - f4e5d6a: implement Python harmonizer + unit tests - 9f8e7d6: TS/Go implementations, backpressure shim, docs, changelogs, version bumps

Files/areas changed (high level): - sdk/python/redwood/credential/harmonizer.py (+tests) - sdk/python/redwood/stream/backpressure_shim.py (+tests) - sdk/python/CHANGELOG.md, sdk/python/setup.cfg (version bump) - sdk/typescript/src/credential/harmonizer.ts - sdk/typescript/src/stream/backpressureShim.ts - sdk/typescript/package.json (version bump) - sdk/go/cred/harmonizer.go - sdk/go/stream/adapter_backpressure.go - docs/guides/credential-harmonizer.md - docs/guides/backpressure.md - integration-tests/harmonizer_loadtest.py - ci/workflows/sdk-integration.yml (small test timeout tweak)

Testing and CI: Initial CI run showed a flaky integration test (harmony_load_integration) failing on macOS runners due to timing assumptions; reran the job with increased timeout and the run passed. Summary: - unit: pass - integration (linux): pass - integration (macos): rerun -> pass - lint/typecheck: pass - e2e smoke: pass. CI artifacts include a short flamegraph and a flamegraph diff showing reduced refresh spikes under the load test.

Review conversation (selected): - Marcus Lin (review): "Great direction — harmonizing refreshes should cut down bursts. Two asks: add a small metric for 'harmonizer_suppressed_refreshes' and make TTL jitter configurable with a sane default." - Author (Asha): "Added metric and config with comment describing tradeoffs; default is 5% window with +/- jitter. See latest commit f4e5d6a." - Priya Shah (review): "Backpressure hints look useful, but please avoid adding another public API surface on StreamHandle; prefer a lightweight opt-in shim factory." - Author: "Adjusted per Priya: StreamHandle gains only an opt-in attach_backpressure_shim(factory) helper; core iterator remains unchanged. Docs updated to show both patterns." - Diego Rivera (review): "Can we ensure harmonizer is no-op when using external credential provider (delegated auth)?" - Author: "Harmonizer detects external providers via has_external_provider() hook and becomes a no-op; added unit test case." - Marcus (approval): "LGTM after CI green and one nit on doc wording."

Author responses: - Fixed doc nit; added test coverage for delegated providers; added metric counter and a brief note about observability in docs.

Performance notes: Load test (1k simulated clients, TTLs uniformly distributed in a 60s window) shows peak refresh requests reduced by ~87% and tail retry-induced 503s reduced by ~68%. Memory use under heavy slow consumers dropped ~22% when using backpressure shim with prefetch=2. Benchmarks included in ci/artifacts/refresh-spike-flamegraphs.zip.

Compatibility and migration notes: - Feature is opt-in. Existing SDK behavior unchanged until caller enables the harmonizer or attaches the backpressure shim. - If customers wire a remote leader/consensus provider into the harmonizer hook, they should ensure single-leader semantics; docs contain a recipe for HashiCorp Consul and a small TTL-coordination example.

Merge: Squash and merge after CI green and approvals. Rollout plan: staged SDK releases over the next two weeks; docs live immediately.

Related work: references ENG-3321 (coordination), ENG-3330 (stream observability), SUP-1209 (customer incident where simultaneous token rollovers caused brief degradation).

Compatibility and migration notes: - Feature is opt-in. Existing SDK behavior unchanged until caller enables the harmonizer or attaches the backpressure shim. - If customers wire a remote leader/consensus provider into the harmonizer hook, they should ensure single-leader semantics; docs contain a recipe for HashiCorp Consul and a small TTL-coordination example.

Merge: Squash and merge after CI green and approvals. Rollout plan: staged SDK releases over the next two weeks; docs live immediately.

Related work: references ENG-3321 (coordination), ENG-3330 (stream observability), SUP-1209 (customer incident where simultaneous token rollovers caused brief degradation).

title: Implement KMS circuit breaker + fallback mode to prevent retry storms during KMS incidents
preview:
Context: During recent KMS throttling/timeouts (see SUP-19377), application-side retries amplified load and created a request storm against KMS, worsening tail latency and elevating 5xx. We’ve already standardized timeouts/retries in the KMS wrapper (PR #18421); this change adds circuit breaking + a documented degrade/fallback policy so we fail fast and shed load instead of stampeding KMS.

What this PR does:
- Adds a per-key (key ARN) circuit breaker in the shared KMS client wrapper with CLOSED/OPEN/HALF_OPEN states.
- Introduces a fallback mode policy for decrypt/envelope operations:
  - If circuit is OPEN: do not call KMS; attempt to serve from in-memory DEK cache if present (bounded TTL, non-persistent); otherwise fail fast with a mapped KMSUnavailable error (no retries).
  - Encrypt/new key generation paths: fail fast when OPEN (no cache fallback), returning a distinct error class to avoid queuing / retry loops.
- Caps concurrency to KMS with a small in-flight semaphore (prevents spikes during HALF_OPEN).
- Emits metrics + logs needed for on-call triage:
  - redwood.kms.circuit_breaker.state (gauge)
  - redwood.kms.circuit_breaker.open_total / half_open_total (counters)
  - redwood.kms.circuit_breaker.blocked_total (counter)
  - Structured log fields: kms.key_arn, kms.op, kms.error_category, kms.circuit_state, kms.circuit_open_reason
- Adds unit tests with fake clock to validate: state transitions, half-open probe behavior, and that OPEN short-circuits before any retry logic.
- Documents the operational behavior in-code (module docs + inline comment block) with explicit non-goals: we are not changing cryptographic primitives, not persisting plaintext key material, and not altering key rotation semantics.

SRE-requested changes included here:
- HALF_OPEN uses jittered probe scheduling to avoid synchronized thundering herd after an incident clears.
- OPEN duration is bounded; we re-check via controlled probes rather than staying OPEN indefinitely.
- Separate breaker keys for decrypt vs metadata calls (avoid a metadata blip taking down decrypt).
- Explicit error mapping for OPEN-short-circuit -> KMS_CIRCUIT_OPEN (distinct from timeout), and we do not apply caller retry policy to this class.

Config (defaults are safe for prod):
- kms.circuit_breaker.enabled=true
- kms.circuit_breaker.failure_threshold=8 (rolling window)
- kms.circuit_breaker.window=30s
- kms.circuit_breaker.open_cooldown=45s (+/- 20% jitter)
- kms.circuit_breaker.half_open_max_in_flight=2
- kms.circuit_breaker.cache_fallback_ttl=5m (decrypt only; bounded, memory-only)

Rollout notes:
- This is behind a feature flag kms.circuit_breaker.enabled (on by default in staging; gradual enable in prod per-region).
- Please coordinate with ENG-4828 (dashboard/alerts) before broad prod enablement.

Test plan:
- Unit tests for breaker transitions and short-circuit behavior.
- Staging: simulate KMS 429/timeout using the fault injection harness; verify: request rate to KMS drops sharply once OPEN, p95 latency stabilizes, and error responses are consistent and actionable.

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Update runbook playbook examples to include KMS_CIRCUIT_OPEN and recommended mitigations (ENG-4821 docs workstream).
- Extend integration tests to assert breaker behavior under sustained throttling (ENG-4825).

Screenshots / evidence:
- Added a short “expected graphs” note to help validate in staging: look for breaker_open_total increments and a drop in kms.request_total during simulated failures.

title: Add audit events for image channel/version changes on fleet pools
preview:
## Context
As part of the provision-to-ready automation rollout, we are standardizing golden image channels (candidate -> stable) and pinning pool image versions. During the us-east/A100 staged rollout we had a quarantine spike and had to quickly determine who/what changed image configuration on the affected pool(s). Today those changes are visible via config history in a few places, but they are not captured as first-class audit events.

This PR emits explicit audit events whenever a pool’s image channel or pinned image version changes (including promotions and rollbacks). These events are designed to be consumable by SecOps/compliance tooling and also useful during incident response/postmortems.

## What’s included
- New audit event type: `fleet.pool.image_config_changed`
- Events emitted on:
  - `image_channel` change (e.g., `candidate` -> `stable`)
  - pinned `image_version` change (e.g., `gpuimg-2025.10.21-1` -> `gpuimg-2025.10.23-2`)
  - explicit “clear pin” operations (pin removed)
- Captured fields:
  - `pool_id`, `region`, `pool_type` (dedicated/shared)
  - `before` + `after` (channel + version)
  - `actor` (service account or user), `actor_type`
  - `reason` (freeform, required for manual changes)
  - `request_id`, `source` (api/automation), and `git_sha` of the control-plane deploy
- Emission is centralized in the pool update pathway (so we don’t miss changes from future callers)
- Guardrails:
  - No per-node identifiers in event payload
  - Size capped + reason truncated (server-side)
  - Event emission is “best effort” but failures are surfaced in metrics and logs

## Why this way
- Compliance needs an immutable record of configuration changes and the identity that performed them.
- Incident response needs to quickly answer: what changed, when, by whom, and what was the intended reason.
- We intentionally avoid logging the entire pool spec to prevent accidental leakage and keep events stable.

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - channel-only changes
  - version-only changes
  - channel+version changes
  - no-op updates (no event)
  - actor attribution for service-to-service calls
- Integration test with the audit sink in `dev` using a fake request id.

## Rollout / operational notes
- Event volume expected to be low (pool config changes are infrequent).
- If the audit sink is degraded, pool updates continue; we increment `audit_emit_failures_total{event_type=...}` and log at WARN with request id.
- Follow-up: wire these events into the readiness UI “change history” panel (out of scope for this PR).

Refs: ENG-4826 (image channels), ENG-4822 (readiness state machine control-plane work).

title: Audit log: emit model change events + internal admin query endpoints
preview:
Problem: Support/Security need authoritative, queryable audit history for model metadata changes (create/update/promote/deprecate/rollback) to answer enterprise escalations (e.g., “what changed, who did it, when, and in which deployment?”) and to satisfy audit/retention expectations for Private/VPC. Today we emit some control-plane events for changelog generation, but they’re not shaped as audit events, not consistently retained, and there’s no internal support surface to query by model/version/workspace.

This PR adds (1) first-class audit log events for model metadata and promotion lifecycle, and (2) an internal admin-only query API used by Support/Compliance to retrieve the audit timeline. Customer-visible release notes remain generated elsewhere; this is the compliance/support trace.

Scope:
- Emit audit events on: model_version.created, model_version.metadata_updated, model_version.promoted, model_version.deprecated, model_version.rollback_initiated, model_version.rollback_completed.
- Ensure events include actor + request context (service/user), are redact-safe (no internal-only fields leak), and are linkable to model IDs/version IDs.
- Add internal admin endpoints to query audit history with pagination and filters; gated behind RBAC permission `auditlog:read:model_changes` and additionally `admin_only=true` (no customer roles).
- Wire audit events into the existing AuditLog pipeline (retention, SIEM export) with a dedicated event namespace `model_registry.*`.

Non-goals:
- Customer subscriptions/notifications.
- Rewriting historical events (we’ll have partial backfill in a follow-up; this PR is forward-looking).

Event contract (high level):
- `event_type`: one of the above
- `event_id`: UUID (idempotency keyed by (request_id, event_type, object_id))
- `timestamp`
- `actor`: {type=user|service, id, email (if user), service_name (if service)}
- `workspace_id` (nullable for internal catalog ops)
- `object`: {kind=model_version, model_id, model_slug, version_id, version_semver}
- `deployment_context`: {hosted|dedicated|private, region (optional), private_cluster_id (optional)}
- `change_summary`: human-readable, redaction-safe summary
- `diff`: stored as internal-only JSON in the audit store; API returns a redacted view unless caller has `auditlog:read:model_changes_internal`

Admin API:
- GET /internal/admin/audit/model-changes?model_id=&version_id=&workspace_id=&event_type=&after=&limit=
- GET /internal/admin/audit/model-changes/{event_id}
Both endpoints are available only to `RedwoodEmployee` principals and service accounts in the `support-tooling` group.

Notes on redaction:
- We never include `internal_notes`, `embargo_reason`, or raw metadata blobs in the default response.
- For internal security/compliance roles, we return a structured `diff_preview` containing only approved fields (field allowlist).

Follow-ups:
- Add UI in Support Admin (separate PR) + docs/runbook for incident responders.
- Backfill best-effort events for the last 30 days using changelog generator store.

Refs:
- Linear: ENG-9828 (audit log events for model metadata changes), ENG-9825 (redaction rules)
- Jira: INT-11903 (support admin access request), SUP-28429 (customer reports output regression after silent model change)
- Related PRs: pr-18435 (control-plane change events), pr-18447 (console APIs), pr-18452 (CI preview), pr-18441 (generator templates)

Checklist:
- [x] Security review requested (Marcus)
- [x] RBAC policy updated + tests
- [x] Event payloads validated against audit schema
- [x] Load test: 1k events/min sustained for 10 min w/ no dropped events
- [x] Runbook snippet added to internal docs (link in comments)
- [x] Metrics + alerts added for event ingestion lag

title: Hotfix: prevent rollout UI from rendering internal-only model release notes for non-admin roles
preview:
Context: During the rollouts UI dogfood of the new model change history / release notes surfaces, we found a case where internal-only note fields (e.g. `internal_summary`, `internal_risk_assessment`, and links to internal runbooks) could appear in the Console for standard (non-admin) roles. This is not expected and violates the public/internal separation we agreed on in ENG-9825.

Root cause: The Rollouts `ReleaseNotesCard` component was rendering the `details` payload verbatim when present. Under certain navigation paths (rollout timeline -> "Change details" -> "Release notes"), the UI was using an internal-capable response type and the renderer did not gate the internal sections on RBAC. While the backend should also enforce this (and does for the public feed), the UI should not trust the presence of fields.

Fix (hotfix / defense-in-depth): (1) Gate all internal-only sections in the Console renderer behind an explicit `canViewInternalReleaseNotes` permission check; (2) add a safe "public projection" mapping that drops all `internal_*` fields by default before rendering; (3) add a regression test that logs in as a standard role and asserts internal-only copy never appears, even when the API response contains internal fields.

Notes: This PR intentionally stays scoped to Console UI + its local API client typing. Follow-up work to further harden the backend response shaping is tracked separately (ENG-9864) but not required for the immediate leak.

Rollout plan: Merge ASAP, deploy with the next Console hotfix train. No schema changes.

Risk: Low. Changes are additive and limited to UI rendering + tests.

Checklist: - [x] Added regression coverage - [x] Verified in staging with standard role + admin role - [x] Confirmed no user-visible copy changes for public release notes
